  100|       |static void inv_iso(fe u_out, fe w_out, const fe u, const fe w)
  101|   242k|{
  102|       |    // 4u
  103|   242k|    fe_dbl(u_out, u);
  104|   242k|    fe_dbl(u_out, u_out);
  105|       |    // 2w
  106|   242k|    fe_dbl(w_out, w);
  107|   242k|};
  108|       |
  109|       |static void inv_psi1(fe e_out, fe u_out, fe w_out, const fe e, const fe u, const fe w)
  110|   161k|{
  111|   161k|    fe e_res, u_res, w_res;
  112|       |
  113|   161k|    fe tt;
  114|   161k|    bool cc = sqrt_ext(tt, u);
  115|   161k|    fe_copy(w_res, tt);
  116|   161k|    fe w_;
  117|   161k|    fe_copy(w_, w);
  118|   161k|    fe_copy(e_res, e);
  119|       |
  120|   161k|    if (!cc)
  121|  86.4k|    {
  122|  86.4k|        fe tt_sq;
  123|  86.4k|        fe_sq(tt_sq, tt);
  124|  86.4k|        fe neg_u_dbl;
  125|  86.4k|        fe_dbl(neg_u_dbl, u);
  126|  86.4k|        fe_neg(neg_u_dbl, neg_u_dbl);
  127|  86.4k|        if (fe_compare(tt_sq, neg_u_dbl)) {
  128|  42.3k|            fe_mul(tt, tt, fe_sqrtm1);
  129|  42.3k|        }
  130|       |
  131|  86.4k|        fe_mul(w_, w, tt);
  132|       |
  133|  86.4k|        fe e_sq;
  134|  86.4k|        fe_sq(e_sq, e);
  135|  86.4k|        fe_mul(w_res, fe_msqrt2b, e_sq);
  136|       |
  137|  86.4k|        fe_mul(e_res, e_res, tt);
  138|  86.4k|    }
  139|       |
  140|   161k|    fe w_res_sq;
  141|   161k|    fe_sq(w_res_sq, w_res);
  142|       |
  143|   161k|    fe e_res_sq;
  144|   161k|    fe_sq(e_res_sq, e_res);
  145|       |
  146|   161k|    fe A_e_sq;
  147|   161k|    fe_mul(A_e_sq, fe_a0, e_res_sq);
  148|       |
  149|   161k|    fe w_res_w;
  150|   161k|    fe_mul(w_res_w, w_res, w_);
  151|       |
  152|   161k|    fe_sub(u_res, w_res_sq, A_e_sq);
  153|   161k|    fe_reduce(u_res, u_res);
  154|   161k|    fe_sub(u_res, u_res, w_res_w);
  155|   161k|    fe_mul(u_res, u_res, fe_inv2);
  156|       |
  157|   161k|    fe_copy(e_out, e_res);
  158|   161k|    fe_copy(u_out, u_res);
  159|   161k|    fe_copy(w_out, w_res);
  160|   161k|};
  161|       |
  162|       |static bool inv_psi2(fe u_out, fe w_out, const fe e, const fe u, const fe w)
  163|   161k|{
  164|   161k|    fe u_res, w_res;
  165|       |
  166|   161k|    if (!fcmp_pp::sqrt(w_res, u))
  167|     49|        return false;
  168|   161k|    fe e_sq;
  169|   161k|    fe_sq(e_sq, e);
  170|   161k|    fe Ap_e_sq;
  171|   161k|    fe_mul(Ap_e_sq, fe_ap, e_sq);
  172|       |
  173|   161k|    fe w_res_w;
  174|   161k|    fe_mul(w_res_w, w_res, w);
  175|       |
  176|   161k|    fe_sub(u_res, u, Ap_e_sq);
  177|   161k|    fe_reduce(u_res, u_res);
  178|   161k|    fe_sub(u_res, u_res, w_res_w);
  179|   161k|    fe_mul(u_res, u_res, fe_inv2);
  180|       |
  181|   161k|    fe_copy(u_out, u_res);
  182|   161k|    fe_copy(w_out, w_res);
  183|       |
  184|   161k|    return true;
  185|   161k|};

  190|  80.7k|bool mul8_is_identity(const ge_p3 &point) {

  200|       |// https://github.com/kayabaNerve/fcmp-plus-plus/blob/94744c5324e869a9483bbbd93a864e108304bf76/crypto/divisors/src/tests/torsion_check.rs
  201|       |// Returns true if point is torsion free
  202|       |// Pre-condition: point is a valid point and point*8 not equal to identity
  203|       |// WARNING1: this approach needs to be carefully vetted academically and audited
  204|       |// before it can be used in production.
  205|       |// WARNING2: since fe_add and fe_sub expect the input fe's to be within a
  206|       |// smaller domain than the output fe, we sometimes need to "reduce" a field elem
  207|       |// to chain calls to fe_add and fe_sub. Notice all calls to fe_reduce.
  208|  80.7k|bool torsion_check_vartime(const ge_p3 &point) {
  209|       |    //assert(!mul8_is_identity(point));
  210|       |
  211|       |    // ed to wei
  212|  80.7k|    fe e, u, w;
  213|  80.7k|    {
  214|  80.7k|        fe z_plus_ed_y, z_minus_ed_y;
  215|  80.7k|        fe_add(z_plus_ed_y, fe_one, point.Y);
  216|  80.7k|        fe_sub(z_minus_ed_y, fe_one, point.Y);
  217|       |
  218|       |        // e
  219|  80.7k|        fe_mul(e, z_minus_ed_y, point.X);
  220|       |        // u
  221|  80.7k|        fe_mul(u, fe_a_sub_d, z_plus_ed_y);
  222|  80.7k|        fe_mul(u, u, point.X);
  223|  80.7k|        fe_mul(u, u, e);
  224|       |        // w
  225|  80.7k|        fe_dbl(w, z_minus_ed_y);
  226|  80.7k|    }
  227|       |
  228|       |    //assert(check_e_u_w(e, u, w));
  229|       |
  230|       |    // Torsion check
  231|   242k|    for (int i = 0; i < 2; ++i) {
  232|   161k|        inv_iso(u, w, u, w);
  233|   161k|        if (!inv_psi2(u, w, e, u, w)) {
  234|     49|            return false;
  235|     49|        }
  236|   161k|        inv_psi1(e, u, w, e, u, w);
  237|       |        //assert(check_e_u_w(e, u, w));
  238|   161k|    }
  239|       |
  240|  80.6k|    fe _;
  241|  80.6k|    inv_iso(u, _, u, w);
  242|       |
  243|  80.6k|    if (!sqrt(u, u)) {
  244|      8|        return false;
  245|      8|    }
  246|       |
  247|  80.6k|    return true;

  702|       |    // The precomputed table is assumed to already clear the top bit, so
  703|       |    // `fe_frombytes_strict` may be used directly.
  704|      0|    const uint8_t *bytes = &precomp_table[i * (2 * 32)];

  707|      0|    fe_frombytes_strict(&y, bytes + 32);

  713|      0|    fe_mul_llt(&out->xy2d, &out->xy2d, &d2);
  714|      0|  }

  717|       |  // precomputed elements. This loop does 64 additions and 64 doublings to

  719|      0|  ge_p3_0(h);

  723|      0|    signed char index = 0;

  756|      0|static void table_select(ge_precomp *t, const int pos, const signed char b) {
  757|      0|  uint8_t bnegative = constant_time_msb_w(b);
  758|      0|  uint8_t babs = b - ((bnegative & b) << 1);
  759|       |

  761|      0|      {static_cast<uint8_t>(constant_time_is_zero_w(b) & 1)},
  762|      0|      {static_cast<uint8_t>(constant_time_is_zero_w(b) & 1)},
  763|      0|      {0}};

  765|      0|  __asm__("" : "+m"(t_bytes) : /*no inputs*/);

  920|      0|static void slide(signed char *r, const uint8_t *a) {
  921|      0|  int i;
  922|      0|  int b;
  923|      0|  int k;
  924|       |
  925|      0|  for (i = 0; i < 256; ++i) {
  926|      0|    r[i] = 1 & (a[i >> 3] >> (i & 7));
  927|      0|  }
  928|       |
  929|      0|  for (i = 0; i < 256; ++i) {
  930|      0|    if (r[i]) {
  931|      0|      for (b = 1; b <= 6 && i + b < 256; ++b) {
  932|      0|        if (r[i + b]) {
  933|      0|          if (r[i] + (r[i + b] << b) <= 15) {
  934|      0|            r[i] += r[i + b] << b;
  935|      0|            r[i + b] = 0;
  936|      0|          } else if (r[i] - (r[i + b] << b) >= -15) {
  937|      0|            r[i] -= r[i + b] << b;
  938|      0|            for (k = i + b; k < 256; ++k) {
  939|      0|              if (!r[k]) {
  940|      0|                r[k] = 1;
  941|      0|                break;
  942|      0|              }
  943|      0|              r[k] = 0;
  944|      0|            }
  945|      0|          } else {
  946|      0|            break;
  947|      0|          }
  948|      0|        }
  949|      0|      }
  950|      0|    }
  951|      0|  }

 1032|       |
 1033|       |// The set of scalars is \Z/l
 1034|       |// where l = 2^252 + 27742317777372353535851937790883648493.
 1035|       |
 1036|       |// Input:
 1037|       |//   s[0]+256*s[1]+...+256^63*s[63] = s
 1038|       |//
 1039|       |// Output:
 1040|       |//   s[0]+256*s[1]+...+256^31*s[31] = s mod l
 1041|       |//   where l = 2^252 + 27742317777372353535851937790883648493.
 1042|       |//   Overwrites s in place.
 1043|      0|void bssl::x25519_sc_reduce(uint8_t s[64]) {

 1865|      0|void ED25519_keypair(uint8_t out_public_key[32], uint8_t out_private_key[64]) {
 1866|      0|  uint8_t seed[32];
 1867|      0|  RAND_bytes(seed, 32);
 1868|      0|  ED25519_keypair_from_seed(out_public_key, out_private_key, seed);
 1869|      0|}
 1870|       |
 1871|       |int ED25519_sign(uint8_t out_sig[64], const uint8_t *message,
 1872|      0|                 size_t message_len, const uint8_t private_key[64]) {
 1873|       |  // NOTE: The documentation on this function says that it returns zero on
 1874|       |  // allocation failure. While that can't happen with the current
 1875|       |  // implementation, we want to reserve the ability to allocate in this
 1876|       |  // implementation in the future.
 1877|       |
 1878|      0|  uint8_t az[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH];
 1879|      0|  SHA512(private_key, 32, az);
 1880|       |
 1881|      0|  az[0] &= 248;
 1882|      0|  az[31] &= 63;
 1883|      0|  az[31] |= 64;
 1884|       |
 1885|      0|  SHA512_CTX hash_ctx;
 1886|      0|  SHA512_Init(&hash_ctx);
 1887|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, az + 32, 32);
 1888|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, message, message_len);
 1889|      0|  uint8_t nonce[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH];
 1890|      0|  SHA512_Final(nonce, &hash_ctx);
 1891|       |
 1892|      0|  x25519_sc_reduce(nonce);
 1893|      0|  ge_p3 R;
 1894|      0|  x25519_ge_scalarmult_base(&R, nonce);
 1895|      0|  ge_p3_tobytes(out_sig, &R);
 1896|       |
 1897|      0|  SHA512_Init(&hash_ctx);
 1898|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, out_sig, 32);
 1899|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, private_key + 32, 32);
 1900|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, message, message_len);
 1901|      0|  uint8_t hram[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH];
 1902|      0|  SHA512_Final(hram, &hash_ctx);
 1903|       |
 1904|      0|  x25519_sc_reduce(hram);
 1905|      0|  sc_muladd(out_sig + 32, hram, az, nonce);
 1906|       |
 1907|       |  // The signature is computed from the private key, but is public.
 1908|      0|  CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(out_sig, 64);
 1909|      0|  return 1;
 1910|      0|}
 1911|       |
 1912|       |int ED25519_verify(const uint8_t *message, size_t message_len,
 1913|      0|                   const uint8_t signature[64], const uint8_t public_key[32]) {
 1914|      0|  ge_p3 A;
 1915|      0|  if ((signature[63] & 224) != 0 ||
 1916|      0|      !x25519_ge_frombytes_vartime(&A, public_key)) {
 1917|      0|    return 0;
 1918|      0|  }
 1919|       |
 1920|      0|  fe_loose t;
 1921|      0|  fe_neg(&t, &A.X);
 1922|      0|  fe_carry(&A.X, &t);
 1923|      0|  fe_neg(&t, &A.T);
 1924|      0|  fe_carry(&A.T, &t);
 1925|       |
 1926|      0|  uint8_t pkcopy[32];
 1927|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(pkcopy, public_key, 32);
 1928|      0|  uint8_t rcopy[32];
 1929|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(rcopy, signature, 32);
 1930|      0|  uint8_t scopy[32];
 1931|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(scopy, signature + 32, 32);
 1932|       |
 1933|       |  // https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc8032#section-5.1.7 requires that s be in
 1934|       |  // the range [0, order) in order to prevent signature malleability.
 1935|       |
 1936|       |  // kOrder is the order of Curve25519 in little-endian form.
 1937|      0|  static const uint64_t kOrder[4] = {
 1938|      0|      UINT64_C(0x5812631a5cf5d3ed),
 1939|      0|      UINT64_C(0x14def9dea2f79cd6),
 1940|      0|      0,
 1941|      0|      UINT64_C(0x1000000000000000),
 1942|      0|  };
 1943|      0|  for (size_t i = 3;; i--) {
 1944|      0|    uint64_t word = CRYPTO_load_u64_le(scopy + i * 8);
 1945|      0|    if (word > kOrder[i]) {
 1946|      0|      return 0;
 1947|      0|    } else if (word < kOrder[i]) {
 1948|      0|      break;
 1949|      0|    } else if (i == 0) {
 1950|      0|      return 0;
 1951|      0|    }
 1952|      0|  }
 1953|       |
 1954|      0|  SHA512_CTX hash_ctx;
 1955|      0|  SHA512_Init(&hash_ctx);
 1956|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, signature, 32);
 1957|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, public_key, 32);
 1958|      0|  SHA512_Update(&hash_ctx, message, message_len);
 1959|      0|  uint8_t h[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH];
 1960|      0|  SHA512_Final(h, &hash_ctx);
 1961|       |
 1962|      0|  x25519_sc_reduce(h);
 1963|       |
 1964|      0|  ge_p2 R;
 1965|      0|  ge_double_scalarmult_vartime(&R, h, &A, scopy);
 1966|       |
 1967|      0|  uint8_t rcheck[32];
 1968|      0|  x25519_ge_tobytes(rcheck, &R);
 1969|       |
 1970|      0|  return CRYPTO_memcmp(rcheck, rcopy, sizeof(rcheck)) == 0;
 1971|      0|}
 1972|       |
 1973|       |void ED25519_keypair_from_seed(uint8_t out_public_key[32],
 1974|       |                               uint8_t out_private_key[64],
 1975|      0|                               const uint8_t seed[32]) {
 1976|      0|  uint8_t az[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH];
 1977|      0|  SHA512(seed, 32, az);
 1978|       |
 1979|      0|  az[0] &= 248;
 1980|      0|  az[31] &= 127;
 1981|      0|  az[31] |= 64;
 1982|       |
 1983|      0|  ge_p3 A;
 1984|      0|  x25519_ge_scalarmult_base(&A, az);
 1985|      0|  ge_p3_tobytes(out_public_key, &A);
 1986|       |  // The public key is derived from the private key, but it is public.
 1987|      0|  CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(out_public_key, 32);
 1988|       |
 1989|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(out_private_key, seed, 32);
 1990|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(out_private_key + 32, out_public_key, 32);
 1991|      0|}
 1992|       |
 1993|       |
 1994|       |static void x25519_scalar_mult_generic(uint8_t out[32],
 1995|       |                                       const uint8_t scalar[32],
 1996|      0|                                       const uint8_t point[32]) {
 1997|      0|  fe x1, x2, z2, x3, z3, tmp0, tmp1;
 1998|      0|  fe_loose x2l, z2l, x3l, tmp0l, tmp1l;
 1999|       |
 2000|      0|  uint8_t e[32];
 2001|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(e, scalar, 32);
 2002|      0|  e[0] &= 248;
 2003|      0|  e[31] &= 127;
 2004|      0|  e[31] |= 64;
 2005|       |
 2006|       |  // The following implementation was transcribed to Coq and proven to
 2007|       |  // correspond to unary scalar multiplication in affine coordinates given that
 2008|       |  // x1 != 0 is the x coordinate of some point on the curve. It was also checked
 2009|       |  // in Coq that doing a ladderstep with x1 = x3 = 0 gives z2' = z3' = 0, and z2
 2010|       |  // = z3 = 0 gives z2' = z3' = 0. The statement was quantified over the
 2011|       |  // underlying field, so it applies to Curve25519 itself and the quadratic
 2012|       |  // twist of Curve25519. It was not proven in Coq that prime-field arithmetic
 2013|       |  // correctly simulates extension-field arithmetic on prime-field values.
 2014|       |  // The decoding of the byte array representation of e was not considered.
 2015|       |  // Specification of Montgomery curves in affine coordinates:
 2016|       |  // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Spec/MontgomeryCurve.v#L27>
 2017|       |  // Proof that these form a group that is isomorphic to a Weierstrass curve:
 2018|       |  // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/AffineProofs.v#L35>
 2019|       |  // Coq transcription and correctness proof of the loop (where scalarbits=255):
 2020|       |  // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZ.v#L118>
 2021|       |  // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZProofs.v#L278>
 2022|       |  // preconditions: 0 <= e < 2^255 (not necessarily e < order), fe_invert(0) = 0
 2023|      0|  fe_frombytes(&x1, point);
 2024|      0|  fe_1(&x2);
 2025|      0|  fe_0(&z2);
 2026|      0|  fe_copy(&x3, &x1);
 2027|      0|  fe_1(&z3);
 2028|       |
 2029|      0|  unsigned swap = 0;
 2030|      0|  int pos;
 2031|      0|  for (pos = 254; pos >= 0; --pos) {
 2032|       |    // loop invariant as of right before the test, for the case where x1 != 0:
 2033|       |    //   pos >= -1; if z2 = 0 then x2 is nonzero; if z3 = 0 then x3 is nonzero
 2034|       |    //   let r := e >> (pos+1) in the following equalities of projective points:
 2035|       |    //   to_xz (r*P)     === if swap then (x3, z3) else (x2, z2)
 2036|       |    //   to_xz ((r+1)*P) === if swap then (x2, z2) else (x3, z3)
 2037|       |    //   x1 is the nonzero x coordinate of the nonzero point (r*P-(r+1)*P)
 2038|      0|    unsigned b = 1 & (e[pos / 8] >> (pos & 7));
 2039|      0|    swap ^= b;
 2040|      0|    fe_cswap(&x2, &x3, swap);
 2041|      0|    fe_cswap(&z2, &z3, swap);
 2042|      0|    swap = b;
 2043|       |    // Coq transcription of ladderstep formula (called from transcribed loop):
 2044|       |    // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZ.v#L89>
 2045|       |    // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZProofs.v#L131>
 2046|       |    // x1 != 0
 2047|       |    // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZProofs.v#L217>
 2048|       |    // x1  = 0
 2049|       |    // <https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/2456d821825521f7e03e65882cc3521795b0320f/src/Curves/Montgomery/XZProofs.v#L147>
 2050|      0|    fe_sub(&tmp0l, &x3, &z3);
 2051|      0|    fe_sub(&tmp1l, &x2, &z2);
 2052|      0|    fe_add(&x2l, &x2, &z2);
 2053|      0|    fe_add(&z2l, &x3, &z3);
 2054|      0|    fe_mul_tll(&z3, &tmp0l, &x2l);
 2055|      0|    fe_mul_tll(&z2, &z2l, &tmp1l);
 2056|      0|    fe_sq_tl(&tmp0, &tmp1l);
 2057|      0|    fe_sq_tl(&tmp1, &x2l);
 2058|      0|    fe_add(&x3l, &z3, &z2);
 2059|      0|    fe_sub(&z2l, &z3, &z2);
 2060|      0|    fe_mul_ttt(&x2, &tmp1, &tmp0);
 2061|      0|    fe_sub(&tmp1l, &tmp1, &tmp0);
 2062|      0|    fe_sq_tl(&z2, &z2l);
 2063|      0|    fe_mul121666(&z3, &tmp1l);
 2064|      0|    fe_sq_tl(&x3, &x3l);
 2065|      0|    fe_add(&tmp0l, &tmp0, &z3);
 2066|      0|    fe_mul_ttt(&z3, &x1, &z2);
 2067|      0|    fe_mul_tll(&z2, &tmp1l, &tmp0l);
 2068|      0|  }
 2069|       |  // here pos=-1, so r=e, so to_xz (e*P) === if swap then (x3, z3) else (x2, z2)
 2070|      0|  fe_cswap(&x2, &x3, swap);
 2071|      0|  fe_cswap(&z2, &z3, swap);
 2072|       |
 2073|      0|  fe_invert(&z2, &z2);
 2074|      0|  fe_mul_ttt(&x2, &x2, &z2);
 2075|      0|  fe_tobytes(out, &x2);
 2076|      0|}
 2077|       |
 2078|       |static void x25519_scalar_mult(uint8_t out[32], const uint8_t scalar[32],
 2079|      0|                               const uint8_t point[32]) {
 2080|       |#if defined(BORINGSSL_X25519_NEON)
 2081|       |  if (CRYPTO_is_NEON_capable()) {
 2082|       |    x25519_NEON(out, scalar, point);
 2083|       |    return;
 2084|       |  }
 2085|       |#elif defined(BORINGSSL_FE25519_ADX)
 2086|       |  if (CRYPTO_is_BMI1_capable() && CRYPTO_is_BMI2_capable() &&
 2087|       |      CRYPTO_is_ADX_capable()) {
 2088|       |    x25519_scalar_mult_adx(out, scalar, point);
 2089|       |    return;
 2090|       |  }
 2091|       |#endif
 2092|       |
 2093|      0|  x25519_scalar_mult_generic(out, scalar, point);
 2094|      0|}
 2095|       |
 2096|      0|void X25519_keypair(uint8_t out_public_value[32], uint8_t out_private_key[32]) {
 2097|      0|  RAND_bytes(out_private_key, 32);
 2098|       |
 2099|       |  // All X25519 implementations should decode scalars correctly (see
 2100|       |  // https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7748#section-5). However, if an
 2101|       |  // implementation doesn't then it might interoperate with random keys a
 2102|       |  // fraction of the time because they'll, randomly, happen to be correctly
 2103|       |  // formed.
 2104|       |  //
 2105|       |  // Thus we do the opposite of the masking here to make sure that our private
 2106|       |  // keys are never correctly masked and so, hopefully, any incorrect
 2107|       |  // implementations are deterministically broken.
 2108|       |  //
 2109|       |  // This does not affect security because, although we're throwing away
 2110|       |  // entropy, a valid implementation of scalarmult should throw away the exact
 2111|       |  // same bits anyway.
 2112|      0|  out_private_key[0] |= ~248;
 2113|      0|  out_private_key[31] &= ~64;
 2114|      0|  out_private_key[31] |= ~127;
 2115|       |
 2116|      0|  X25519_public_from_private(out_public_value, out_private_key);
 2117|      0|}
 2118|       |
 2119|       |int X25519(uint8_t out_shared_key[32], const uint8_t private_key[32],
 2120|      0|           const uint8_t peer_public_value[32]) {
 2121|      0|  static const uint8_t kZeros[32] = {0};
 2122|      0|  x25519_scalar_mult(out_shared_key, private_key, peer_public_value);
 2123|       |  // The all-zero output results when the input is a point of small order.
 2124|      0|  return constant_time_declassify_int(
 2125|      0|             CRYPTO_memcmp(kZeros, out_shared_key, 32)) != 0;
 2126|      0|}
 2127|       |
 2128|       |void X25519_public_from_private(uint8_t out_public_value[32],
 2129|      0|                                const uint8_t private_key[32]) {
 2130|       |#if defined(BORINGSSL_X25519_NEON)
 2131|       |  if (CRYPTO_is_NEON_capable()) {
 2132|       |    static const uint8_t kMongomeryBasePoint[32] = {9};
 2133|       |    x25519_NEON(out_public_value, private_key, kMongomeryBasePoint);
 2134|       |    return;
 2135|       |  }
 2136|       |#endif
 2137|       |
 2138|      0|  uint8_t e[32];
 2139|      0|  OPENSSL_memcpy(e, private_key, 32);
 2140|      0|  e[0] &= 248;
 2141|      0|  e[31] &= 127;
 2142|      0|  e[31] |= 64;
 2143|       |
 2144|      0|  ge_p3 A;
 2145|      0|  x25519_ge_scalarmult_base(&A, e);
 2146|       |
 2147|       |  // We only need the u-coordinate of the curve25519 point. The map is
 2148|       |  // u=(y+1)/(1-y). Since y=Y/Z, this gives u=(Z+Y)/(Z-Y).
 2149|      0|  fe_loose zplusy, zminusy;
 2150|      0|  fe zminusy_inv;
 2151|      0|  fe_add(&zplusy, &A.Z, &A.Y);
 2152|      0|  fe_sub(&zminusy, &A.Z, &A.Y);
 2153|      0|  fe_loose_invert(&zminusy_inv, &zminusy);
 2154|      0|  fe_mul_tlt(&zminusy_inv, &zplusy, &zminusy_inv);
 2155|      0|  fe_tobytes(out_public_value, &zminusy_inv);
 2156|      0|  CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(out_public_value, 32);
 2157|      0|}

   25|       |// This header will eventually become the interface between BCM and the
   26|       |// rest of libcrypto. More cleanly separating the two is still a work in
   27|       |// progress (see https://crbug.com/boringssl/722) so, at the moment, we
   28|       |// consider this no different from any other header in BCM.
   29|       |//
   30|       |// Over time, calls from libcrypto to BCM will all move to this header
   31|       |// and the separation will become more meaningful.
   32|       |
   33|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
   34|       |
   35|       |// Enumerated types for return values from bcm functions, both infallible
   36|       |// and fallible functions. Two success values are used to correspond to the
   37|       |// FIPS service indicator. For the moment, the official service indicator
   38|       |// remains the counter, not these values. Once we fully transition to
   39|       |// these return values from bcm we will change that.
   40|       |enum class bcm_infallible_t {
   41|       |  approved,
   42|       |  not_approved,
   43|       |};
   44|       |
   45|       |enum class bcm_status_t {
   46|       |  approved,
   47|       |  not_approved,
   48|       |  failure,
   49|       |};
   50|       |typedef enum bcm_status_t bcm_status;
   51|       |typedef enum bcm_infallible_t bcm_infallible;
   52|       |
   53|      0|inline int bcm_success(bcm_status status) {
   54|      0|  return status == bcm_status::approved || status == bcm_status::not_approved;
   55|      0|}
   56|       |
   57|      0|inline bcm_status_t bcm_as_approved_status(int result) {
   58|      0|  return result ? bcm_status::approved : bcm_status::failure;
   59|      0|}
   60|       |
   61|      0|inline bcm_status_t bcm_as_not_approved_status(int result) {
   62|      0|  return result ? bcm_status::not_approved : bcm_status::failure;
   63|      0|}

   65|       |// Random number generator.
   66|       |

   68|       |

   70|       |// TODO(bbe): disentangle this value which is used to calculate the size of the
   71|       |// stack buffer in RAND_need entropy based on a calculation.

   80|       |// BCM_rand_bytes_hwrng attempts to fill `out` with `len` bytes of entropy from
   81|       |// the CPU hardware random number generator if one is present.
   82|       |// bcm_status_approved is returned on success, and a failure status is
   83|       |// returned otherwise.

   92|       |// SHA-1
   93|       |
   94|       |// BCM_sha1_init initialises `sha`.
   95|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha1_init(SHA_CTX *sha);
   96|       |
   97|       |// SHA1_transform is a low-level function that performs a single, SHA-1
   98|       |// block transformation using the state from `sha` and `SHA_CBLOCK` bytes from
   99|       |// `block`.
  100|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha1_transform(SHA_CTX *c, const uint8_t data[SHA_CBLOCK]);
  101|       |
  102|       |// BCM_sha1_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  103|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha1_update(SHA_CTX *c, const void *data, size_t len);
  104|       |
  105|       |// BCM_sha1_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  106|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of space.
  107|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha1_final(uint8_t out[SHA_DIGEST_LENGTH], SHA_CTX *c);
  108|       |
  109|       |
  110|       |// BCM_fips_186_2_prf derives `out_len` bytes from `xkey` using the PRF
  111|       |// defined in FIPS 186-2, Appendix 3.1, with change notice 1 applied. The b
  112|       |// parameter is 160 and seed, XKEY, is also 160 bits. The optional XSEED user
  113|       |// input is all zeros.
  114|       |//
  115|       |// The PRF generates a sequence of 320-bit numbers. Each number is encoded as a
  116|       |// 40-byte string in big-endian and then concatenated to form `out`. If
  117|       |// `out_len` is not a multiple of 40, the result is truncated. This matches the
  118|       |// construction used in Section 7 of RFC 4186 and Section 7 of RFC 4187.
  119|       |//
  120|       |// This PRF is based on SHA-1, a weak hash function, and should not be used
  121|       |// in new protocols. It is provided for compatibility with some legacy EAP
  122|       |// methods.
  123|       |bcm_infallible BCM_fips_186_2_prf(uint8_t *out, size_t out_len,
  124|       |                                  const uint8_t xkey[SHA_DIGEST_LENGTH]);
  125|       |
  126|       |
  127|       |// SHA-224
  128|       |
  129|       |// BCM_sha224_unit initialises `sha`.
  130|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha224_init(SHA256_CTX *sha);
  131|       |
  132|       |// BCM_sha224_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  133|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha224_update(SHA256_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
  134|       |
  135|       |// BCM_sha224_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  136|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of
  137|       |// space. It aborts on programmer error.
  138|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha224_final(uint8_t out[SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  139|       |                                SHA256_CTX *sha);
  140|       |
  141|       |
  142|       |// SHA-256
  143|       |
  144|       |// BCM_sha256_init initialises `sha`.
  145|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha256_init(SHA256_CTX *sha);
  146|       |
  147|       |// BCM_sha256_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  148|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha256_update(SHA256_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
  149|       |
  150|       |// BCM_sha256_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  151|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of
  152|       |// space. It aborts on programmer error.
  153|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha256_final(uint8_t out[SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  154|       |                                SHA256_CTX *sha);
  155|       |
  156|       |// BCM_sha256_transform is a low-level function that performs a single, SHA-256
  157|       |// block transformation using the state from `sha` and `SHA256_CBLOCK` bytes
  158|       |// from `block`.
  159|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha256_transform(SHA256_CTX *sha,
  160|       |                                    const uint8_t block[SHA256_CBLOCK]);
  161|       |
  162|       |// BCM_sha256_transform_blocks is a low-level function that takes `num_blocks` *
  163|       |// `SHA256_CBLOCK` bytes of data and performs SHA-256 transforms on it to update
  164|       |// `state`.
  165|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha256_transform_blocks(uint32_t state[8],
  166|       |                                           const uint8_t *data,
  167|       |                                           size_t num_blocks);
  168|       |
  169|       |
  170|       |// SHA-384.
  171|       |
  172|       |// BCM_sha384_init initialises `sha`.
  173|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha384_init(SHA512_CTX *sha);
  174|       |
  175|       |// BCM_sha384_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  176|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha384_update(SHA512_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
  177|       |
  178|       |// BCM_sha384_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  179|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA384_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of
  180|       |// space. It may abort on programmer error.
  181|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha384_final(uint8_t out[SHA384_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  182|       |                                SHA512_CTX *sha);
  183|       |
  184|       |
  185|       |// SHA-512.
  186|       |
  187|       |// BCM_sha512_init initialises `sha`.
  188|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_init(SHA512_CTX *sha);
  189|       |
  190|       |// BCM_sha512_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  191|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_update(SHA512_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
  192|       |
  193|       |// BCM_sha512_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  194|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of
  195|       |// space.
  196|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_final(uint8_t out[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  197|       |                                SHA512_CTX *sha);
  198|       |
  199|       |// BCM_sha512_transform is a low-level function that performs a single, SHA-512
  200|       |// block transformation using the state from `sha` and `SHA512_CBLOCK` bytes
  201|       |// from `block`.
  202|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_transform(SHA512_CTX *sha,
  203|       |                                    const uint8_t block[SHA512_CBLOCK]);
  204|       |
  205|       |
  206|       |// SHA-512-256
  207|       |//
  208|       |// See https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/FIPS/NIST.FIPS.180-4.pdf section 5.3.6
  209|       |
  210|       |// BCM_sha512_256_init initialises `sha`.
  211|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_256_init(SHA512_CTX *sha);
  212|       |
  213|       |// BCM_sha512_256_update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha`.
  214|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_256_update(SHA512_CTX *sha, const void *data,
  215|       |                                     size_t len);
  216|       |
  217|       |// BCM_sha512_256_final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting
  218|       |// digest to `out`, which must have at least `SHA512_256_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of
  219|       |// space. It may abort on programmer error.
  220|       |bcm_infallible BCM_sha512_256_final(uint8_t out[SHA512_256_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  221|       |                                    SHA512_CTX *sha);
  222|       |
  223|       |
  224|       |// ML-DSA
  225|       |//
  226|       |// Where not commented, these functions have the same signature as the
  227|       |// corresponding public function.
  228|       |
  229|       |// BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES is the number of bytes of uniformly
  230|       |// random entropy necessary to generate a signature in randomized mode.
  231|       |#define BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES 32
  232|       |
  233|       |// BCM_MLDSA65_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES is the number of bytes in an encoded ML-DSA-65
  234|       |// private key.
  235|       |#define BCM_MLDSA65_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES 4032
  236|       |
  237|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_generate_key(
  238|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA65_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  239|       |    uint8_t out_seed[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES], MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key);
  240|       |
  241|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_private_key_from_seed(
  242|       |    MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key, const uint8_t seed[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  243|       |
  244|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_public_from_private(
  245|       |    MLDSA65_public_key *out_public_key, const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key);
  246|       |
  247|       |// BCM_mldsa65_public_of_private returns the public half of `private_key`.
  248|       |const MLDSA65_public_key *BCM_mldsa65_public_of_private(
  249|       |    const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key);
  250|       |
  251|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  252|       |BCM_mldsa65_check_key_fips(MLDSA65_private_key *private_key);
  253|       |
  254|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_generate_key_fips(
  255|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA65_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  256|       |    uint8_t out_seed[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES], MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key);
  257|       |
  258|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_private_key_from_seed_fips(
  259|       |    MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key, const uint8_t seed[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  260|       |
  261|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  262|       |BCM_mldsa65_sign(uint8_t out_encoded_signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  263|       |                 const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key, const uint8_t *msg,
  264|       |                 size_t msg_len, const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len);
  265|       |
  266|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_verify(
  267|       |    const MLDSA65_public_key *public_key,
  268|       |    const uint8_t signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES], const uint8_t *msg,
  269|       |    size_t msg_len, const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len);
  270|       |
  271|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void BCM_mldsa65_prehash_init(
  272|       |    MLDSA65_prehash *out_prehash_ctx, const MLDSA65_public_key *public_key,
  273|       |    const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len);
  274|       |
  275|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void BCM_mldsa65_prehash_update(
  276|       |    MLDSA65_prehash *inout_prehash_ctx, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len);

  279|       |    uint8_t out_msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES], MLDSA65_prehash *inout_prehash_ctx);
  280|       |

  283|       |    const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key,
  284|       |    const uint8_t msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES]);
  285|       |
  286|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_verify_message_representative(
  287|       |    const MLDSA65_public_key *public_key,
  288|       |    const uint8_t signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  289|       |    const uint8_t msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES]);
  290|       |
  291|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  292|       |BCM_mldsa65_marshal_public_key(CBB *out, const MLDSA65_public_key *public_key);
  293|       |
  294|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  295|       |BCM_mldsa65_parse_public_key(MLDSA65_public_key *public_key, CBS *in);
  296|       |
  297|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  298|       |BCM_mldsa65_parse_private_key(MLDSA65_private_key *private_key, CBS *in);
  299|       |
  300|       |// BCM_mldsa65_generate_key_external_entropy generates a public/private key pair
  301|       |// using the given seed, writes the encoded public key to
  302|       |// `out_encoded_public_key` and sets `out_private_key` to the private key.
  303|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_generate_key_external_entropy(
  304|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA65_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  305|       |    MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key,
  306|       |    const uint8_t entropy[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  307|       |
  308|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_generate_key_external_entropy_fips(
  309|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA65_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  310|       |    MLDSA65_private_key *out_private_key,
  311|       |    const uint8_t entropy[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  312|       |
  313|       |// BCM_mldsa5_sign_internal signs `msg` using `private_key` and writes the
  314|       |// signature to `out_encoded_signature`. The `context_prefix` and `context` are
  315|       |// prefixed to the message, in that order, before signing. The `randomizer`
  316|       |// value can be set to zero bytes in order to make a deterministic signature, or
  317|       |// else filled with entropy for the usual `MLDSA_sign` behavior.
  318|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_sign_internal(
  319|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  320|       |    const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len,
  321|       |    const uint8_t *context_prefix, size_t context_prefix_len,
  322|       |    const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len,
  323|       |    const uint8_t randomizer[BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES]);
  324|       |
  325|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_sign_mu_internal(
  326|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  327|       |    const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key,
  328|       |    const uint8_t msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES],
  329|       |    const uint8_t randomizer[BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES]);
  330|       |
  331|       |// BCM_mldsa5_verify_internal verifies that `encoded_signature` is a valid
  332|       |// signature of `msg` by `public_key`. The `context_prefix` and `context` are
  333|       |// prefixed to the message before verification, in that order.
  334|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_verify_internal(
  335|       |    const MLDSA65_public_key *public_key,
  336|       |    const uint8_t encoded_signature[MLDSA65_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  337|       |    const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len, const uint8_t *context_prefix,
  338|       |    size_t context_prefix_len, const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len);
  339|       |
  340|       |// BCM_mldsa65_marshal_private_key serializes `private_key` to `out` in the
  341|       |// NIST format for ML-DSA-65 private keys.
  342|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa65_marshal_private_key(
  343|       |    CBB *out, const MLDSA65_private_key *private_key);
  344|       |
  345|       |// BCM_mldsa65_public_keys_equal returns one if `a` and `b` are equal and zero
  346|       |// otherwise.
  347|       |int BCM_mldsa65_public_keys_equal(const MLDSA65_public_key *a,
  348|       |                                  const MLDSA65_public_key *b);

  524|       |    const uint8_t msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES]);
  525|       |
  526|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  527|       |BCM_mldsa44_marshal_public_key(CBB *out, const MLDSA44_public_key *public_key);
  528|       |
  529|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  530|       |BCM_mldsa44_parse_public_key(MLDSA44_public_key *public_key, CBS *in);
  531|       |
  532|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  533|       |BCM_mldsa44_parse_private_key(MLDSA44_private_key *private_key, CBS *in);
  534|       |
  535|       |// BCM_mldsa44_generate_key_external_entropy generates a public/private key pair
  536|       |// using the given seed, writes the encoded public key to
  537|       |// `out_encoded_public_key` and sets `out_private_key` to the private key.
  538|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_generate_key_external_entropy(
  539|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA44_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  540|       |    MLDSA44_private_key *out_private_key,
  541|       |    const uint8_t entropy[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  542|       |
  543|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_generate_key_external_entropy_fips(
  544|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLDSA44_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  545|       |    MLDSA44_private_key *out_private_key,
  546|       |    const uint8_t entropy[MLDSA_SEED_BYTES]);
  547|       |
  548|       |// BCM_mldsa44_sign_internal signs `msg` using `private_key` and writes the
  549|       |// signature to `out_encoded_signature`. The `context_prefix` and `context` are
  550|       |// prefixed to the message, in that order, before signing. The `randomizer`
  551|       |// value can be set to zero bytes in order to make a deterministic signature, or
  552|       |// else filled with entropy for the usual `MLDSA_sign` behavior.
  553|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_sign_internal(
  554|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_signature[MLDSA44_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  555|       |    const MLDSA44_private_key *private_key, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len,
  556|       |    const uint8_t *context_prefix, size_t context_prefix_len,
  557|       |    const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len,
  558|       |    const uint8_t randomizer[BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES]);
  559|       |
  560|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_sign_mu_internal(
  561|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_signature[MLDSA44_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  562|       |    const MLDSA44_private_key *private_key,
  563|       |    const uint8_t msg_rep[MLDSA_MU_BYTES],
  564|       |    const uint8_t randomizer[BCM_MLDSA_SIGNATURE_RANDOMIZER_BYTES]);
  565|       |
  566|       |// BCM_mldsa44_verify_internal verifies that `encoded_signature` is a valid
  567|       |// signature of `msg` by `public_key`. The `context_prefix` and `context` are
  568|       |// prefixed to the message before verification, in that order.
  569|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_verify_internal(
  570|       |    const MLDSA44_public_key *public_key,
  571|       |    const uint8_t encoded_signature[MLDSA44_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  572|       |    const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len, const uint8_t *context_prefix,
  573|       |    size_t context_prefix_len, const uint8_t *context, size_t context_len);
  574|       |
  575|       |// BCM_mldsa44_marshal_private_key serializes `private_key` to `out` in the
  576|       |// NIST format for ML-DSA-44 private keys.
  577|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mldsa44_marshal_private_key(
  578|       |    CBB *out, const MLDSA44_private_key *private_key);
  579|       |
  580|       |// BCM_mldsa44_public_keys_equal returns one if `a` and `b` are equal and zero
  581|       |// otherwise.
  582|       |int BCM_mldsa44_public_keys_equal(const MLDSA44_public_key *a,
  583|       |                                  const MLDSA44_public_key *b);

  586|       |// ML-KEM
  587|       |//
  588|       |// Where not commented, these functions have the same signature as the
  589|       |// corresponding public function.
  590|       |
  591|       |// BCM_MLKEM_ENCAP_ENTROPY is the number of bytes of uniformly random entropy
  592|       |// necessary to encapsulate a secret. The entropy will be leaked to the
  593|       |// decapsulating party.
  594|       |#define BCM_MLKEM_ENCAP_ENTROPY 32
  595|       |
  596|       |// BCM_MLKEM768_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES is the length of the data produced by

  598|       |#define BCM_MLKEM768_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES 2400
  599|       |
  600|       |// BCM_MLKEM1024_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES is the length of the data produced by
  601|       |// `BCM_mlkem1024_marshal_private_key`.
  602|       |#define BCM_MLKEM1024_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES 3168
  603|       |

  641|       |// BCM_mlkem768_public_keys_equal returns one if `a` and `b` are equal and zero

  649|       |// BCM_mlkem768_parse_private_key parses a private key, in NIST's format for
  650|       |// private keys, from `in` and writes the result to `out_private_key`. It
  651|       |// returns one on success or zero on parse error or if there are trailing bytes
  652|       |// in `in`. This format is verbose and should be avoided. Private keys should be
  653|       |// stored as seeds and parsed using `BCM_mlkem768_private_key_from_seed`.
  654|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status
  655|       |BCM_mlkem768_parse_private_key(MLKEM768_private_key *out_private_key, CBS *in);
  656|       |
  657|       |// BCM_mlkem768_generate_key_external_seed is a deterministic function to create
  658|       |// a pair of ML-KEM-768 keys, using the supplied seed. The seed needs to be
  659|       |// uniformly random. This function should only be used for tests; regular
  660|       |// callers should use the non-deterministic `BCM_mlkem768_generate_key`
  661|       |// directly.
  662|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_mlkem768_generate_key_external_seed(
  663|       |    uint8_t out_encoded_public_key[MLKEM768_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  664|       |    MLKEM768_private_key *out_private_key,
  665|       |    const uint8_t seed[MLKEM_SEED_BYTES]);
  666|       |
  667|       |// BCM_mlkem768_encap_external_entropy behaves like `MLKEM768_encap`, but uses
  668|       |// `MLKEM_ENCAP_ENTROPY` bytes of `entropy` for randomization. The decapsulating
  669|       |// side will be able to recover `entropy` in full. This function should only be
  670|       |// used for tests, regular callers should use the non-deterministic
  671|       |// `BCM_mlkem768_encap` directly.
  672|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_mlkem768_encap_external_entropy(
  673|       |    uint8_t out_ciphertext[MLKEM768_CIPHERTEXT_BYTES],

  675|       |    const MLKEM768_public_key *public_key,
  676|       |    const uint8_t entropy[BCM_MLKEM_ENCAP_ENTROPY]);
  677|       |
  678|       |// BCM_mlkem768_marshal_private_key serializes `private_key` to `out` in the
  679|       |// NIST format for ML-KEM-768 private keys. (Note that one can also save just
  680|       |// the seed value produced by `BCM_mlkem768_generate_key`, which is
  681|       |// significantly smaller.)
  682|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_mlkem768_marshal_private_key(
  683|       |    CBB *out, const MLKEM768_private_key *private_key);

  765|       |
  766|       |
  767|       |// SLH-DSA
  768|       |
  769|       |// Output length of the hash function.
  770|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_N 16
  771|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_N 32
  772|       |
  773|       |// The number of bytes at the beginning of M', the augmented message, before the
  774|       |// context.
  775|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_M_PRIME_HEADER_LEN 2
  776|       |
  777|       |// SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES is the number of bytes in an
  778|       |// SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s public key.
  779|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES 32
  780|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES 64
  781|       |
  782|       |// BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES is the number of bytes in an
  783|       |// SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s private key.
  784|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES 64
  785|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES 128
  786|       |
  787|       |// BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_SIGNATURE_BYTES is the number of bytes in an
  788|       |// SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signature.
  789|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_SIGNATURE_BYTES 7856
  790|       |#define BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_SIGNATURE_BYTES 49856
  791|       |
  792|       |// BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key_from_seed generates an SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s
  793|       |// key pair from a 48-byte seed and writes the result to `out_public_key` and
  794|       |// `out_secret_key`.
  795|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key_from_seed(
  796|       |    uint8_t out_public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  797|       |    uint8_t out_secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  798|       |    const uint8_t seed[3 * BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_N]);
  799|       |
  800|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_slhdsa_shake_256f_generate_key_from_seed(
  801|       |    uint8_t out_public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  802|       |    uint8_t out_secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  803|       |    const uint8_t seed[3 * BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_N]);
  804|       |
  805|       |// BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key_from_seed_fips does the same thing as
  806|       |// `BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key_from_seed` but implements the required
  807|       |// second check before generating a key by testing for nullptr arguments.
  808|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key_from_seed_fips(
  809|       |    uint8_t out_public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  810|       |    uint8_t out_secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  811|       |    const uint8_t seed[3 * BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_N]);
  812|       |
  813|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_slhdsa_shake_256f_generate_key_from_seed_fips(
  814|       |    uint8_t out_public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  815|       |    uint8_t out_secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  816|       |    const uint8_t seed[3 * BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_N]);
  817|       |
  818|       |// BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_sign_internal acts like `SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_sign` but
  819|       |// accepts an explicit entropy input, which can be PK.seed (bytes 32..48 of
  820|       |// the private key) to generate deterministic signatures. It also takes the
  821|       |// input message in three parts so that the "internal" version of the signing
  822|       |// function, from section 9.2, can be implemented. The `header` argument may be
  823|       |// NULL to omit it.
  824|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_sign_internal(
  825|       |    uint8_t out_signature[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  826|       |    const uint8_t secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  827|       |    const uint8_t header[BCM_SLHDSA_M_PRIME_HEADER_LEN], const uint8_t *context,
  828|       |    size_t context_len, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len,
  829|       |    const uint8_t entropy[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_N]);
  830|       |
  831|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_slhdsa_shake_256f_sign_internal(
  832|       |    uint8_t out_signature[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_SIGNATURE_BYTES],
  833|       |    const uint8_t secret_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES],
  834|       |    const uint8_t header[BCM_SLHDSA_M_PRIME_HEADER_LEN], const uint8_t *context,
  835|       |    size_t context_len, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len,
  836|       |    const uint8_t entropy[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_N]);
  837|       |
  838|       |// BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_verify_internal acts like `SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_verify` but
  839|       |// takes the input message in three parts so that the "internal" version of the
  840|       |// verification function, from section 9.3, can be implemented. The `header`
  841|       |// argument may be NULL to omit it.
  842|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_verify_internal(
  843|       |    const uint8_t *signature, size_t signature_len,
  844|       |    const uint8_t public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  845|       |    const uint8_t header[BCM_SLHDSA_M_PRIME_HEADER_LEN], const uint8_t *context,
  846|       |    size_t context_len, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len);
  847|       |
  848|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_status BCM_slhdsa_shake_256f_verify_internal(
  849|       |    const uint8_t *signature, size_t signature_len,
  850|       |    const uint8_t public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHAKE_256F_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  851|       |    const uint8_t header[BCM_SLHDSA_M_PRIME_HEADER_LEN], const uint8_t *context,
  852|       |    size_t context_len, const uint8_t *msg, size_t msg_len);
  853|       |
  854|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT bcm_infallible BCM_slhdsa_sha2_128s_generate_key(
  855|       |    uint8_t out_public_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES],
  856|       |    uint8_t out_private_key[BCM_SLHDSA_SHA2_128S_PRIVATE_KEY_BYTES]);
  857|       |

    1|       |// Copyright 1999-2016 The OpenSSL Project Authors. All Rights Reserved.
    2|       |//
    3|       |// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
    4|       |// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.

   12|       |// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   13|       |// limitations under the License.
   14|       |
   15|       |#ifndef OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_FIPSMODULE_DIGEST_MD32_COMMON_H
   16|       |#define OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_FIPSMODULE_DIGEST_MD32_COMMON_H
   17|       |
   18|       |#include <openssl/base.h>
   19|       |#include <openssl/span.h>
   20|       |
   21|       |#include <assert.h>
   22|       |
   23|       |#include "../../internal.h"
   24|       |
   25|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
   26|       |
   27|       |
   28|       |// This is a generic 32-bit "collector" for message digest algorithms. It
   29|       |// collects input character stream into chunks of 32-bit values and invokes the
   30|       |// block function that performs the actual hash calculations.
   31|       |//
   32|       |// To make use of this mechanism, the hash context should be defined with the
   33|       |// following parameters.
   34|       |//
   35|       |//     typedef struct <name>_state_st {
   36|       |//       uint32_t h[<chaining length> / sizeof(uint32_t)];
   37|       |//       uint32_t Nl, Nh;
   38|       |//       uint8_t data[<block size>];
   39|       |//       unsigned num;
   40|       |//       ...
   41|       |//     } <NAME>_CTX;
   42|       |//
   43|       |// <chaining length> is the output length of the hash in bytes, before
   44|       |// any truncation (e.g. 64 for SHA-224 and SHA-256, 128 for SHA-384 and
   45|       |// SHA-512).
   46|       |//
   47|       |// `h` is the hash state and is updated by a function of type
   48|       |// `crypto_md32_block_func`. `data` is the partial unprocessed block and has
   49|       |// `num` bytes. `Nl` and `Nh` maintain the number of bits processed so far.
   50|       |//
   51|       |// The template parameter is then a traits struct defined as follows:
   52|       |//
   53|       |//     struct HashTraits {
   54|       |//       // HashContext is the hash type defined above.
   55|       |//       using HashContext = <NAME>_CTX;
   56|       |//
   57|       |//       // kBlockSize is the block size of the hash function.
   58|       |//       static constexpr size_t kBlockSize = <block size>;
   59|       |//
   60|       |//       // kLengthIsBigEndian determines whether the final length is encoded in
   61|       |//       // big or little endian.
   62|       |//       static constexpr bool kLengthIsBigEndian = ...;
   63|       |//
   64|       |//       // HashBlocks incorporates `num_blocks` blocks of input from `data`
   65|       |//       // into `state`. It is assumed the caller has sized `state` and `data`
   66|       |//       // for the hash function.
   67|       |//       static void HashBlocks(uint32_t *state, const uint8_t *data,
   68|       |//                              size_t num_blocks) {
   69|       |//         <name>_block_data_order(state, data, num_blocks);
   70|       |//       }
   71|       |//     };
   72|       |//
   73|       |// The reason for this formulation is to encourage the compiler to specialize
   74|       |// all the code for the block size and block function.
   75|       |
   76|       |// crypto_md32_update hashes `in` to `ctx`.
   77|       |template <typename Traits>
   78|       |inline void crypto_md32_update(typename Traits::HashContext *ctx,
   79|   160k|                               Span<const uint8_t> in) {
   80|   160k|  static_assert(Traits::kBlockSize == sizeof(ctx->data), "block size is wrong");
   81|   160k|  if (in.empty()) {
   82|      1|    return;
   83|      1|  }
   84|       |
   85|   160k|  uint32_t l = ctx->Nl + ((static_cast<uint32_t>(in.size())) << 3);
   86|   160k|  if (l < ctx->Nl) {
   87|       |    // Handle carries.
   88|      0|    ctx->Nh++;
   89|      0|  }
   90|   160k|  ctx->Nh += static_cast<uint32_t>(in.size() >> 29);
   91|   160k|  ctx->Nl = l;
   92|       |
   93|   160k|  size_t n = ctx->num;
   94|   160k|  if (n != 0) {
   95|      0|    if (in.size() >= Traits::kBlockSize ||
   96|      0|        in.size() + n >= Traits::kBlockSize) {
   97|      0|      OPENSSL_memcpy(ctx->data + n, in.data(), Traits::kBlockSize - n);
   98|      0|      Traits::HashBlocks(ctx->h, ctx->data, 1);
   99|      0|      in = in.subspan(Traits::kBlockSize - n);
  100|      0|      ctx->num = 0;
  101|       |      // Keep `data` zeroed when unused.
  102|      0|      OPENSSL_memset(ctx->data, 0, Traits::kBlockSize);
  103|      0|    } else {
  104|      0|      OPENSSL_memcpy(ctx->data + n, in.data(), in.size());
  105|      0|      ctx->num += static_cast<unsigned>(in.size());
  106|      0|      return;
  107|      0|    }
  108|      0|  }
  109|       |
  110|   160k|  n = in.size() / Traits::kBlockSize;
  111|   160k|  if (n > 0) {
  112|      1|    Traits::HashBlocks(ctx->h, in.data(), n);
  113|      1|    in = in.subspan(n * Traits::kBlockSize);
  114|      1|  }
  115|       |
  116|   160k|  if (!in.empty()) {
  117|   160k|    ctx->num = static_cast<unsigned>(in.size());
  118|   160k|    OPENSSL_memcpy(ctx->data, in.data(), in.size());
  119|   160k|  }
  120|   160k|}
  121|       |
  122|       |// crypto_md32_final incorporates the partial block and trailing length into the
  123|       |// digest state in `ctx`. The trailing length is encoded in little-endian if
  124|       |// `is_big_endian` is zero and big-endian otherwise. `data` must be a buffer of
  125|       |// length `block_size` with the first `*num` bytes containing a partial block.
  126|       |// `Nh` and `Nl` contain the total number of bits processed. On return, this
  127|       |// function clears the partial block in `data` and
  128|       |// `*num`.
  129|       |//
  130|       |// This function does not serialize `h` into a final digest. This is the
  131|       |// responsibility of the caller.
  132|       |template <typename Traits>
  133|   160k|inline void crypto_md32_final(typename Traits::HashContext *ctx) {
  134|   160k|  static_assert(Traits::kBlockSize == sizeof(ctx->data), "block size is wrong");
  135|       |  // `data` always has room for at least one byte. A full block would have
  136|       |  // been consumed.
  137|   160k|  size_t n = ctx->num;
  138|   160k|  assert(n < Traits::kBlockSize);
  139|   160k|  ctx->data[n] = 0x80;
  140|   160k|  n++;
  141|       |
  142|       |  // Fill the block with zeros if there isn't room for a 64-bit length.
  143|   160k|  if (n > Traits::kBlockSize - 8) {
  144|      1|    OPENSSL_memset(ctx->data + n, 0, Traits::kBlockSize - n);
  145|      1|    n = 0;
  146|      1|    Traits::HashBlocks(ctx->h, ctx->data, 1);
  147|      1|  }
  148|   160k|  OPENSSL_memset(ctx->data + n, 0, Traits::kBlockSize - 8 - n);
  149|       |
  150|       |  // Append a 64-bit length to the block and process it.
  151|   160k|  if constexpr (Traits::kLengthIsBigEndian) {
  152|   160k|    CRYPTO_store_u32_be(ctx->data + Traits::kBlockSize - 8, ctx->Nh);
  153|   160k|    CRYPTO_store_u32_be(ctx->data + Traits::kBlockSize - 4, ctx->Nl);
  154|       |  } else {
  155|       |    CRYPTO_store_u32_le(ctx->data + Traits::kBlockSize - 8, ctx->Nl);
  156|       |    CRYPTO_store_u32_le(ctx->data + Traits::kBlockSize - 4, ctx->Nh);
  157|       |  }
  158|   160k|  Traits::HashBlocks(ctx->h, ctx->data, 1);
  159|   160k|  ctx->num = 0;
  160|   160k|  OPENSSL_memset(ctx->data, 0, Traits::kBlockSize);
  161|   160k|}
  162|       |
  163|       |
  164|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
  165|       |
  166|       |#endif  // OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_FIPSMODULE_DIGEST_MD32_COMMON_H

   14|       |
   15|       |#ifndef OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_FIPSMODULE_SERVICE_INDICATOR_INTERNAL_H
   16|       |#define OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_FIPSMODULE_SERVICE_INDICATOR_INTERNAL_H
   17|       |
   18|       |#include <openssl/base.h>
   19|       |
   20|       |
   21|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
   22|       |
   23|       |// FIPS_service_indicator_before_call and `FIPS_service_indicator_after_call`
   24|       |// both currently return the same local thread counter which is slowly
   25|       |// incremented whenever approved services are called. The
   26|       |// `CALL_SERVICE_AND_CHECK_APPROVED` macro is strongly recommended over calling
   27|       |// these functions directly.
   28|       |//
   29|       |// `FIPS_service_indicator_before_call` is intended to be called immediately
   30|       |// before an approved service, while `FIPS_service_indicator_after_call` should
   31|       |// be called immediately after. If the values returned from these two functions
   32|       |// are not equal, this means that the service called in between is deemed to be
   33|       |// approved. If the values are still the same, this means the counter has not
   34|       |// been incremented, and the service called is not approved for FIPS.
   35|       |//
   36|       |// In non-FIPS builds, `FIPS_service_indicator_before_call` always returns zero
   37|       |// and `FIPS_service_indicator_after_call` always returns one. Thus calls always
   38|       |// appear to be approved. This is intended to simplify testing.
   39|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT uint64_t FIPS_service_indicator_before_call();
   40|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT uint64_t FIPS_service_indicator_after_call();
   41|       |
   42|       |#if defined(BORINGSSL_FIPS)
   43|       |
   44|       |// FIPS_service_indicator_update_state records that an approved service has been
   45|       |// invoked.
   46|       |void FIPS_service_indicator_update_state();
   47|       |
   48|       |// FIPS_service_indicator_lock_state and `FIPS_service_indicator_unlock_state`
   49|       |// stop `FIPS_service_indicator_update_state` from actually updating the service
   50|       |// indicator. This is used when a primitive calls a potentially approved
   51|       |// primitive to avoid false positives. For example, just because a key
   52|       |// generation calls `BCM_rand_bytes` (and thus the approved DRBG) doesn't mean
   53|       |// that the key generation operation itself is approved.
   54|       |//
   55|       |// This lock nests: i.e. locking twice is fine so long as each lock is paired
   56|       |// with an unlock. If the (64-bit) counter overflows, the process aborts.
   57|       |void FIPS_service_indicator_lock_state();
   58|       |void FIPS_service_indicator_unlock_state();
   59|       |
   60|       |// The following functions may call `FIPS_service_indicator_update_state` if
   61|       |// their parameter specifies an approved operation.
   62|       |
   63|       |void AEAD_GCM_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_AEAD_CTX *ctx);
   64|       |void AEAD_CCM_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_AEAD_CTX *ctx);
   65|       |void EC_KEY_keygen_verify_service_indicator(const EC_KEY *eckey);
   66|       |void ECDH_verify_service_indicator(const EC_KEY *ec_key);
   67|       |void EVP_Cipher_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_CIPHER_CTX *ctx);
   68|       |void EVP_DigestSign_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_MD_CTX *ctx);
   69|       |void EVP_DigestVerify_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_MD_CTX *ctx);
   70|       |void HMAC_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_MD *evp_md);
   71|       |void TLSKDF_verify_service_indicator(const EVP_MD *dgst);
   72|       |
   73|       |#else
   74|       |
   75|       |// Service indicator functions are no-ops in non-FIPS builds.
   76|       |
   77|   160k|inline void FIPS_service_indicator_update_state() {}
   78|      0|inline void FIPS_service_indicator_lock_state() {}
   79|      0|inline void FIPS_service_indicator_unlock_state() {}
   80|       |
   81|       |inline void AEAD_GCM_verify_service_indicator(
   82|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_AEAD_CTX *ctx) {}
   83|       |
   84|       |inline void AEAD_CCM_verify_service_indicator(
   85|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_AEAD_CTX *ctx) {}
   86|       |
   87|       |inline void EC_KEY_keygen_verify_service_indicator(
   88|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EC_KEY *eckey) {}
   89|       |
   90|       |inline void ECDH_verify_service_indicator(
   91|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EC_KEY *ec_key) {}
   92|       |
   93|       |inline void EVP_Cipher_verify_service_indicator(
   94|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_CIPHER_CTX *ctx) {}
   95|       |
   96|       |inline void EVP_DigestSign_verify_service_indicator(
   97|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_MD_CTX *ctx) {}
   98|       |
   99|       |inline void EVP_DigestVerify_verify_service_indicator(
  100|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_MD_CTX *ctx) {}
  101|       |
  102|       |inline void HMAC_verify_service_indicator(
  103|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_MD *evp_md) {}

  105|       |inline void TLSKDF_verify_service_indicator(
  106|      0|    [[maybe_unused]] const EVP_MD *dgst) {}

  108|   160k|                                      SHA256_CTX *c) {
  109|       |  // Ideally we would assert `sha->md_len` is `SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH` tomatch the
  110|       |  // size hint, but calling code often pairs `SHA224_Init` with `SHA256_Final`
  111|       |  // and expects `sha->md_len` to carry the size over.
  112|       |  //
  113|       |  // TODO(davidben): Add an assert and fix code to match them up.
  114|   160k|  sha256_final_impl(out, c->md_len, c);
  115|   160k|  return bcm_infallible::approved;
  116|   160k|}

  145|       |// See FIPS 180-4, section 4.1.2.
  146|       |#define Sigma0(x)                                       \
  147|  10.2M|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 2) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 13) ^ \
  148|  10.2M|   CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 22))
  149|       |#define Sigma1(x)                                       \
  150|  10.2M|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 6) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 11) ^ \
  151|  10.2M|   CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 25))
  152|       |#define sigma0(x) \
  153|  7.70M|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 7) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 18) ^ ((x) >> 3))
  154|       |#define sigma1(x) \
  155|  7.70M|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 17) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u32((x), 19) ^ ((x) >> 10))
  156|       |
  157|  10.2M|#define Ch(x, y, z) (((x) & (y)) ^ ((~(x)) & (z)))
  158|  10.2M|#define Maj(x, y, z) (((x) & (y)) ^ ((x) & (z)) ^ ((y) & (z)))
  159|       |
  160|       |#define ROUND_00_15(i, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h)   \
  161|  10.2M|  do {                                           \
  162|  10.2M|    T1 += h + Sigma1(e) + Ch(e, f, g) + K256[i]; \
  163|  10.2M|    h = Sigma0(a) + Maj(a, b, c);                \
  164|  10.2M|    d += T1;                                     \
  165|  10.2M|    h += T1;                                     \
  166|  10.2M|  } while (0)
  167|       |
  168|       |#define ROUND_16_63(i, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, X)      \
  169|  7.70M|  do {                                                 \
  170|  7.70M|    s0 = X[(i + 1) & 0x0f];                            \
  171|  7.70M|    s0 = sigma0(s0);                                   \
  172|  7.70M|    s1 = X[(i + 14) & 0x0f];                           \
  173|  7.70M|    s1 = sigma1(s1);                                   \
  174|  7.70M|    T1 = X[(i) & 0x0f] += s0 + s1 + X[(i + 9) & 0x0f]; \
  175|  7.70M|    ROUND_00_15(i, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);            \
  176|  7.70M|  } while (0)

  178|       |static void sha256_block_data_order_nohw(uint32_t state[8], const uint8_t *data,
  179|   160k|                                         size_t num) {
  180|   160k|  uint32_t a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, s0, s1, T1;
  181|   160k|  uint32_t X[16];
  182|   160k|  int i;
  183|       |
  184|   321k|  while (num--) {
  185|   160k|    a = state[0];
  186|   160k|    b = state[1];
  187|   160k|    c = state[2];
  188|   160k|    d = state[3];
  189|   160k|    e = state[4];
  190|   160k|    f = state[5];
  191|   160k|    g = state[6];
  192|   160k|    h = state[7];
  193|       |
  194|   160k|    T1 = X[0] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  195|   160k|    data += 4;
  196|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(0, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);
  197|   160k|    T1 = X[1] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  198|   160k|    data += 4;
  199|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(1, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g);
  200|   160k|    T1 = X[2] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  201|   160k|    data += 4;
  202|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(2, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f);
  203|   160k|    T1 = X[3] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  204|   160k|    data += 4;
  205|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(3, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e);
  206|   160k|    T1 = X[4] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  207|   160k|    data += 4;
  208|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(4, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d);
  209|   160k|    T1 = X[5] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  210|   160k|    data += 4;
  211|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(5, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c);
  212|   160k|    T1 = X[6] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  213|   160k|    data += 4;
  214|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(6, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b);
  215|   160k|    T1 = X[7] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  216|   160k|    data += 4;
  217|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(7, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a);
  218|   160k|    T1 = X[8] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  219|   160k|    data += 4;
  220|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(8, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);
  221|   160k|    T1 = X[9] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  222|   160k|    data += 4;
  223|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(9, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g);
  224|   160k|    T1 = X[10] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  225|   160k|    data += 4;
  226|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(10, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f);
  227|   160k|    T1 = X[11] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  228|   160k|    data += 4;
  229|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(11, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e);
  230|   160k|    T1 = X[12] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  231|   160k|    data += 4;
  232|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(12, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d);
  233|   160k|    T1 = X[13] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  234|   160k|    data += 4;
  235|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(13, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c);
  236|   160k|    T1 = X[14] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  237|   160k|    data += 4;
  238|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(14, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b);
  239|   160k|    T1 = X[15] = CRYPTO_load_u32_be(data);
  240|   160k|    data += 4;
  241|   160k|    ROUND_00_15(15, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a);
  242|       |
  243|  1.12M|    for (i = 16; i < 64; i += 8) {
  244|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 0, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, X);
  245|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 1, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, X);
  246|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 2, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, X);
  247|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 3, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, X);
  248|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 4, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, X);
  249|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 5, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, X);
  250|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 6, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, X);
  251|   963k|      ROUND_16_63(i + 7, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, X);
  252|   963k|    }
  253|       |
  254|   160k|    state[0] += a;
  255|   160k|    state[1] += b;
  256|   160k|    state[2] += c;
  257|   160k|    state[3] += d;
  258|   160k|    state[4] += e;
  259|   160k|    state[5] += f;
  260|   160k|    state[6] += g;
  261|   160k|    state[7] += h;
  262|   160k|  }
  263|   160k|}

  266|       |
  267|       |static void sha256_block_data_order(uint32_t state[8], const uint8_t *data,
  268|   160k|                                    size_t num) {
  269|       |#if defined(SHA256_ASM_HW)
  270|       |  if (sha256_hw_capable()) {
  271|       |    sha256_block_data_order_hw(state, data, num);
  272|       |    return;
  273|       |  }
  274|       |#endif
  275|       |#if defined(SHA256_ASM_AVX)
  276|       |  if (sha256_avx_capable()) {
  277|       |    sha256_block_data_order_avx(state, data, num);
  278|       |    return;
  279|       |  }
  280|       |#endif
  281|       |#if defined(SHA256_ASM_SSSE3)
  282|       |  if (sha256_ssse3_capable()) {
  283|       |    sha256_block_data_order_ssse3(state, data, num);
  284|       |    return;
  285|       |  }
  286|       |#endif
  287|       |#if defined(SHA256_ASM_NEON)
  288|       |  if (CRYPTO_is_NEON_capable()) {
  289|       |    sha256_block_data_order_neon(state, data, num);
  290|       |    return;
  291|       |  }
  292|       |#endif
  293|   160k|  sha256_block_data_order_nohw(state, data, num);
  294|   160k|}
  295|       |
  296|       |#endif  // !defined(SHA256_ASM)
  297|       |
  298|       |
  299|       |bcm_infallible bssl::BCM_sha256_transform_blocks(uint32_t state[8],
  300|       |                                                 const uint8_t *data,
  301|      0|                                                 size_t num_blocks) {
  302|      0|  if (num_blocks > 0) {
  303|      0|    sha256_block_data_order(state, data, num_blocks);
  304|      0|  }
  305|      0|  return bcm_infallible::approved;

   27|       |// The 32-bit hash algorithms share a common byte-order neutral collector and
   28|       |// padding function implementations that operate on unaligned data,
   29|       |// ../digest/md32_common.h. SHA-512 is the only 64-bit hash algorithm, as of
   30|       |// this writing, so there is no need for a common collector/padding
   31|       |// implementation yet.

   76|      0|  sha->h[5] = UINT64_C(0xbe5e1e2553863992);

   78|      0|  sha->h[7] = UINT64_C(0x0eb72ddc81c52ca2);
   79|       |
   80|      0|  sha->bytes_so_far_low = 0;
   81|      0|  sha->bytes_so_far_high = 0;
   82|      0|  sha->num = 0;
   83|      0|  sha->md_len = SHA512_256_DIGEST_LENGTH;
   84|      0|  return bcm_infallible::approved;
   85|      0|}

  126|       |
  127|       |bcm_infallible bssl::BCM_sha512_update(SHA512_CTX *c, const void *in_data,
  128|      0|                                       size_t len) {
  129|      0|  uint8_t *p = c->p;
  130|      0|  const uint8_t *data = reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t *>(in_data);
  131|       |
  132|      0|  if (len == 0) {
  133|      0|    return bcm_infallible::approved;
  134|      0|  }
  135|       |
  136|      0|  c->bytes_so_far_low += len;
  137|      0|  if (c->bytes_so_far_low < len) {
  138|      0|    c->bytes_so_far_high++;
  139|      0|  }
  140|       |
  141|      0|  if (c->num != 0) {
  142|      0|    size_t n = sizeof(c->p) - c->num;
  143|       |
  144|      0|    if (len < n) {
  145|      0|      OPENSSL_memcpy(p + c->num, data, len);
  146|      0|      c->num += (unsigned int)len;
  147|      0|      return bcm_infallible::approved;
  148|      0|    } else {
  149|      0|      OPENSSL_memcpy(p + c->num, data, n), c->num = 0;
  150|      0|      len -= n;
  151|      0|      data += n;
  152|      0|      sha512_block_data_order(c->h, p, 1);
  153|      0|    }
  154|      0|  }
  155|       |
  156|      0|  if (len >= sizeof(c->p)) {
  157|      0|    sha512_block_data_order(c->h, data, len / sizeof(c->p));
  158|      0|    data += len;
  159|      0|    len %= sizeof(c->p);
  160|      0|    data -= len;
  161|      0|  }
  162|       |
  163|      0|  if (len != 0) {
  164|      0|    OPENSSL_memcpy(p, data, len);
  165|      0|    c->num = (int)len;
  166|      0|  }
  167|       |
  168|      0|  return bcm_infallible::approved;
  169|      0|}
  170|       |
  171|       |bcm_infallible bssl::BCM_sha512_final(uint8_t out[SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH],
  172|      0|                                      SHA512_CTX *sha) {
  173|       |  // Ideally we would assert `sha->md_len` is `SHA512_DIGEST_LENGTH` to match
  174|       |  // the size hint, but calling code often pairs `BCM_sha384_init` with
  175|       |  // `BCM_sha512_final` and expects `sha->md_len` to carry the size over.
  176|       |  //
  177|       |  // TODO(davidben): Add an assert and fix code to match them up.
  178|      0|  sha512_final_impl(out, sha->md_len, sha);
  179|      0|  return bcm_infallible::approved;
  180|      0|}
  181|       |
  182|      0|static void sha512_final_impl(uint8_t *out, size_t md_len, SHA512_CTX *sha) {
  183|      0|  uint8_t *p = sha->p;
  184|      0|  size_t n = sha->num;
  185|       |
  186|      0|  p[n] = 0x80;  // There always is a room for one
  187|      0|  n++;
  188|      0|  if (n > (sizeof(sha->p) - 16)) {
  189|      0|    OPENSSL_memset(p + n, 0, sizeof(sha->p) - n);
  190|      0|    n = 0;
  191|      0|    sha512_block_data_order(sha->h, p, 1);
  192|      0|  }
  193|       |
  194|      0|  OPENSSL_memset(p + n, 0, sizeof(sha->p) - 16 - n);
  195|      0|  const uint64_t Nh = (uint64_t{sha->bytes_so_far_high} << 3) |
  196|      0|                      (sha->bytes_so_far_low >> (64 - 3));
  197|      0|  const uint64_t Nl = sha->bytes_so_far_low << 3;
  198|      0|  CRYPTO_store_u64_be(p + sizeof(sha->p) - 16, Nh);
  199|      0|  CRYPTO_store_u64_be(p + sizeof(sha->p) - 8, Nl);
  200|       |
  201|      0|  sha512_block_data_order(sha->h, p, 1);
  202|       |
  203|      0|  assert(md_len % 8 == 0);
  204|      0|  const size_t out_words = md_len / 8;
  205|      0|  for (size_t i = 0; i < out_words; i++) {
  206|      0|    CRYPTO_store_u64_be(out, sha->h[i]);
  207|      0|    out += 8;
  208|      0|  }
  209|       |
  210|      0|  FIPS_service_indicator_update_state();
  211|      0|}
  212|       |
