  247|       |    UINT64_C(0x90befffa23631e28), UINT64_C(0xa4506cebde82bde9),
  248|       |    UINT64_C(0xbef9a3f7b2c67915), UINT64_C(0xc67178f2e372532b),
  249|       |    UINT64_C(0xca273eceea26619c), UINT64_C(0xd186b8c721c0c207),
  250|       |    UINT64_C(0xeada7dd6cde0eb1e), UINT64_C(0xf57d4f7fee6ed178),
  251|       |    UINT64_C(0x06f067aa72176fba), UINT64_C(0x0a637dc5a2c898a6),
  252|       |    UINT64_C(0x113f9804bef90dae), UINT64_C(0x1b710b35131c471b),
  253|       |    UINT64_C(0x28db77f523047d84), UINT64_C(0x32caab7b40c72493),
  254|       |    UINT64_C(0x3c9ebe0a15c9bebc), UINT64_C(0x431d67c49c100d4c),
  255|       |    UINT64_C(0x4cc5d4becb3e42b6), UINT64_C(0x597f299cfc657e2a),
  256|       |    UINT64_C(0x5fcb6fab3ad6faec), UINT64_C(0x6c44198c4a475817),
  257|       |};
  258|       |
  259|       |#define Sigma0(x)                                        \
  260|      0|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 28) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 34) ^ \
  261|      0|   CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 39))
  262|       |#define Sigma1(x)                                        \
  263|      0|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 14) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 18) ^ \
  264|      0|   CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 41))
  265|       |#define sigma0(x) \
  266|      0|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 1) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 8) ^ ((x) >> 7))
  267|       |#define sigma1(x) \
  268|      0|  (CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 19) ^ CRYPTO_rotr_u64((x), 61) ^ ((x) >> 6))
  269|       |
  270|      0|#define Ch(x, y, z) (((x) & (y)) ^ ((~(x)) & (z)))
  271|      0|#define Maj(x, y, z) (((x) & (y)) ^ ((x) & (z)) ^ ((y) & (z)))
  272|       |
  273|       |
  274|       |#if defined(__i386) || defined(__i386__) || defined(_M_IX86)
  275|       |// This code should give better results on 32-bit CPU with less than
  276|       |// ~24 registers, both size and performance wise...
  277|       |static void sha512_block_data_order_nohw(uint64_t state[8], const uint8_t *in,
  278|       |                                         size_t num) {
  279|       |  uint64_t A, E, T;
  280|       |  uint64_t X[9 + 80], *F;
  281|       |  int i;
  282|       |
  283|       |  while (num--) {
  284|       |    F = X + 80;
  285|       |    A = state[0];
  286|       |    F[1] = state[1];
  287|       |    F[2] = state[2];
  288|       |    F[3] = state[3];
  289|       |    E = state[4];
  290|       |    F[5] = state[5];
  291|       |    F[6] = state[6];
  292|       |    F[7] = state[7];
  293|       |
  294|       |    for (i = 0; i < 16; i++, F--) {
  295|       |      T = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + i * 8);
  296|       |      F[0] = A;
  297|       |      F[4] = E;
  298|       |      F[8] = T;
  299|       |      T += F[7] + Sigma1(E) + Ch(E, F[5], F[6]) + K512[i];
  300|       |      E = F[3] + T;
  301|       |      A = T + Sigma0(A) + Maj(A, F[1], F[2]);
  302|       |    }
  303|       |
  304|       |    for (; i < 80; i++, F--) {
  305|       |      T = sigma0(F[8 + 16 - 1]);
  306|       |      T += sigma1(F[8 + 16 - 14]);
  307|       |      T += F[8 + 16] + F[8 + 16 - 9];
  308|       |
  309|       |      F[0] = A;
  310|       |      F[4] = E;
  311|       |      F[8] = T;
  312|       |      T += F[7] + Sigma1(E) + Ch(E, F[5], F[6]) + K512[i];
  313|       |      E = F[3] + T;
  314|       |      A = T + Sigma0(A) + Maj(A, F[1], F[2]);
  315|       |    }
  316|       |
  317|       |    state[0] += A;
  318|       |    state[1] += F[1];
  319|       |    state[2] += F[2];
  320|       |    state[3] += F[3];
  321|       |    state[4] += E;
  322|       |    state[5] += F[5];
  323|       |    state[6] += F[6];
  324|       |    state[7] += F[7];
  325|       |
  326|       |    in += 16 * 8;
  327|       |  }
  328|       |}
  329|       |
  330|       |#else
  331|       |
  332|       |#define ROUND_00_15(i, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h)   \
  333|      0|  do {                                           \
  334|      0|    T1 += h + Sigma1(e) + Ch(e, f, g) + K512[i]; \
  335|      0|    h = Sigma0(a) + Maj(a, b, c);                \
  336|      0|    d += T1;                                     \
  337|      0|    h += T1;                                     \
  338|      0|  } while (0)
  339|       |
  340|       |#define ROUND_16_80(i, j, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, X)   \
  341|      0|  do {                                                 \
  342|      0|    s0 = X[(j + 1) & 0x0f];                            \
  343|      0|    s0 = sigma0(s0);                                   \
  344|      0|    s1 = X[(j + 14) & 0x0f];                           \
  345|      0|    s1 = sigma1(s1);                                   \
  346|      0|    T1 = X[(j) & 0x0f] += s0 + s1 + X[(j + 9) & 0x0f]; \
  347|      0|    ROUND_00_15(i + j, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);        \
  348|      0|  } while (0)
  349|       |
  350|       |static void sha512_block_data_order_nohw(uint64_t state[8], const uint8_t *in,
  351|      0|                                         size_t num) {
  352|      0|  uint64_t a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, s0, s1, T1;
  353|      0|  uint64_t X[16];
  354|      0|  int i;
  355|       |
  356|      0|  while (num--) {
  357|      0|    a = state[0];
  358|      0|    b = state[1];
  359|      0|    c = state[2];
  360|      0|    d = state[3];
  361|      0|    e = state[4];
  362|      0|    f = state[5];
  363|      0|    g = state[6];
  364|      0|    h = state[7];
  365|       |
  366|      0|    T1 = X[0] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in);
  367|      0|    ROUND_00_15(0, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);
  368|      0|    T1 = X[1] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 8);
  369|      0|    ROUND_00_15(1, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g);
  370|      0|    T1 = X[2] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 2 * 8);
  371|      0|    ROUND_00_15(2, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f);
  372|      0|    T1 = X[3] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 3 * 8);
  373|      0|    ROUND_00_15(3, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e);
  374|      0|    T1 = X[4] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 4 * 8);
  375|      0|    ROUND_00_15(4, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d);
  376|      0|    T1 = X[5] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 5 * 8);
  377|      0|    ROUND_00_15(5, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c);
  378|      0|    T1 = X[6] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 6 * 8);
  379|      0|    ROUND_00_15(6, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b);
  380|      0|    T1 = X[7] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 7 * 8);
  381|      0|    ROUND_00_15(7, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a);
  382|      0|    T1 = X[8] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 8 * 8);
  383|      0|    ROUND_00_15(8, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);
  384|      0|    T1 = X[9] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 9 * 8);
  385|      0|    ROUND_00_15(9, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g);
  386|      0|    T1 = X[10] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 10 * 8);
  387|      0|    ROUND_00_15(10, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f);
  388|      0|    T1 = X[11] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 11 * 8);
  389|      0|    ROUND_00_15(11, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e);
  390|      0|    T1 = X[12] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 12 * 8);
  391|      0|    ROUND_00_15(12, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d);
  392|      0|    T1 = X[13] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 13 * 8);
  393|      0|    ROUND_00_15(13, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c);
  394|      0|    T1 = X[14] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 14 * 8);
  395|      0|    ROUND_00_15(14, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b);
  396|      0|    T1 = X[15] = CRYPTO_load_u64_be(in + 15 * 8);
  397|      0|    ROUND_00_15(15, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a);
  398|       |
  399|      0|    for (i = 16; i < 80; i += 16) {
  400|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 0, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, X);
  401|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 1, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, X);
  402|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 2, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, X);
  403|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 3, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, X);
  404|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 4, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, X);
  405|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 5, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, X);
  406|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 6, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, X);
  407|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 7, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, X);
  408|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 8, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, X);
  409|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 9, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, X);
  410|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 10, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, f, X);
  411|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 11, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, e, X);
  412|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 12, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, d, X);
  413|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 13, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, c, X);
  414|      0|      ROUND_16_80(i, 14, c, d, e, f, g, h, a, b, X);

  177|       |// buffers_alias returns one if `a` and `b` alias and zero otherwise.
  178|       |inline int buffers_alias(const void *a, size_t a_bytes, const void *b,
  179|      0|                         size_t b_bytes) {
  180|      0|  // Cast `a` and `b` to integers. In C, pointer comparisons between unrelated
  181|      0|  // objects are undefined whereas pointer to integer conversions are merely
  182|      0|  // implementation-defined. We assume the implementation defined it in a sane
  183|      0|  // way.
  184|      0|  uintptr_t a_u = (uintptr_t)a;
  185|      0|  uintptr_t b_u = (uintptr_t)b;
  186|      0|  return a_u + a_bytes > b_u && b_u + b_bytes > a_u;
  187|      0|}
  188|       |
  189|       |// spans_alias returns one if `a` and `b` alias, and zero otherwise.
  190|       |template <typename T>
  191|       |inline int spans_alias(Span<const T> a, Span<const T> b) {
  192|       |  return buffers_alias(a.data(), a.size() * sizeof(T), b.data(),
  193|       |                       b.size() * sizeof(T));
  194|       |}
  195|       |
  196|       |// align_pointer returns `ptr`, advanced to `alignment`. `alignment` must be a
  197|       |// power of two, and `ptr` must have at least `alignment - 1` bytes of scratch
  198|       |// space.
  199|      0|inline void *align_pointer(void *ptr, size_t alignment) {
  200|      0|  // `alignment` must be a power of two.
  201|      0|  assert(alignment != 0 && (alignment & (alignment - 1)) == 0);
  202|      0|  // Instead of aligning `ptr` as a `uintptr_t` and casting back, compute the
  203|      0|  // offset and advance in pointer space. C guarantees that casting from pointer
  204|      0|  // to `uintptr_t` and back gives the same pointer, but general
  205|      0|  // integer-to-pointer conversions are implementation-defined. GCC does define
  206|      0|  // it in the useful way, but this makes fewer assumptions.
  207|      0|  uintptr_t offset = (0u - (uintptr_t)ptr) & (alignment - 1);
  208|      0|  ptr = (char *)ptr + offset;
  209|      0|  assert(((uintptr_t)ptr & (alignment - 1)) == 0);
  210|      0|  return ptr;
  211|      0|}

  213|       |
  214|       |// Constant-time utility functions.
  215|       |//
  216|       |// The following methods return a bitmask of all ones (0xff...f) for true and 0
  217|       |// for false. This is useful for choosing a value based on the result of a
  218|       |// conditional in constant time. For example,
  219|       |//
  220|       |// if (a < b) {
  221|       |//   c = a;
  222|       |// } else {
  223|       |//   c = b;
  224|       |// }
  225|       |//
  226|       |// can be written as
  227|       |//
  228|       |// crypto_word_t lt = constant_time_lt_w(a, b);
  229|       |// c = constant_time_select_w(lt, a, b);
  230|       |
  231|       |// crypto_word_t is the type that most constant-time functions use. Ideally we
  232|       |// would like it to be `size_t`, but NaCl builds in 64-bit mode with 32-bit
  233|       |// pointers, which means that `size_t` can be 32 bits when `BN_ULONG` is 64
  234|       |// bits. Since we want to be able to do constant-time operations on a
  235|       |// `BN_ULONG`, `crypto_word_t` is defined as an unsigned value with the native
  236|       |// word length.
  237|       |#if defined(OPENSSL_64_BIT)
  238|       |typedef uint64_t crypto_word_t;
  239|       |#elif defined(OPENSSL_32_BIT)
  240|       |typedef uint32_t crypto_word_t;
  241|       |#else
  242|       |#error "Must define either OPENSSL_32_BIT or OPENSSL_64_BIT"
  243|       |#endif
  244|       |
  245|       |#define CONSTTIME_TRUE_W ~((crypto_word_t)0)
  246|       |#define CONSTTIME_FALSE_W ((crypto_word_t)0)
  247|       |#define CONSTTIME_TRUE_8 ((uint8_t)0xff)
  248|       |#define CONSTTIME_FALSE_8 ((uint8_t)0)
  249|       |
  250|       |// value_barrier_w returns `a`, but prevents GCC and Clang from reasoning about
  251|       |// the returned value. This is used to mitigate compilers undoing constant-time
  252|       |// code, until we can express our requirements directly in the language.
  253|       |//
  254|       |// Note the compiler is aware that `value_barrier_w` has no side effects and
  255|       |// always has the same output for a given input. This allows it to eliminate
  256|       |// dead code, move computations across loops, and vectorize.
  257|      0|inline crypto_word_t value_barrier_w(crypto_word_t a) {
  258|      0|#if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
  259|      0|  __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
  260|      0|#endif
  261|      0|  return a;
  262|      0|}
  263|       |
  264|       |// value_barrier_u32 behaves like `value_barrier_w` but takes a `uint32_t`.
  265|      0|inline uint32_t value_barrier_u32(uint32_t a) {
  266|      0|#if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
  267|      0|  __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
  268|      0|#endif
  269|      0|  return a;
  270|      0|}
  271|       |
  272|       |// value_barrier_u64 behaves like `value_barrier_w` but takes a `uint64_t`.
  273|      0|inline uint64_t value_barrier_u64(uint64_t a) {
  274|      0|#if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
  275|      0|  __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
  276|      0|#endif
  277|      0|  return a;
  278|      0|}
  279|       |
  280|       |// `value_barrier_u8` could be defined as above, but compilers other than
  281|       |// clang seem to still materialize 0x00..00MM instead of reusing 0x??..??MM.
  282|       |
  283|       |// constant_time_msb_w returns the given value with the MSB copied to all the
  284|       |// other bits.
  285|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_msb_w(crypto_word_t a) {
  286|      0|  return 0u - (a >> (sizeof(a) * 8 - 1));
  287|      0|}
  288|       |
  289|       |// constant_time_lt_w returns 0xff..f if a < b and 0 otherwise.
  290|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_lt_w(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  291|      0|  // Consider the two cases of the problem:
  292|      0|  //   msb(a) == msb(b): a < b iff the MSB of a - b is set.
  293|      0|  //   msb(a) != msb(b): a < b iff the MSB of b is set.
  294|      0|  //
  295|      0|  // If msb(a) == msb(b) then the following evaluates as:
  296|      0|  //   msb(a^((a^b)|((a-b)^a))) ==
  297|      0|  //   msb(a^((a-b) ^ a))       ==   (because msb(a^b) == 0)
  298|      0|  //   msb(a^a^(a-b))           ==   (rearranging)
  299|      0|  //   msb(a-b)                      (because ∀x. x^x == 0)
  300|      0|  //
  301|      0|  // Else, if msb(a) != msb(b) then the following evaluates as:
  302|      0|  //   msb(a^((a^b)|((a-b)^a))) ==
  303|      0|  //   msb(a^(𝟙 | ((a-b)^a)))   ==   (because msb(a^b) == 1 and 𝟙
  304|      0|  //                                  represents a value s.t. msb(𝟙) = 1)
  305|      0|  //   msb(a^𝟙)                 ==   (because ORing with 1 results in 1)
  306|      0|  //   msb(b)
  307|      0|  //
  308|      0|  //
  309|      0|  // Here is an SMT-LIB verification of this formula:
  310|      0|  //
  311|      0|  // (define-fun lt ((a (_ BitVec 32)) (b (_ BitVec 32))) (_ BitVec 32)
  312|      0|  //   (bvxor a (bvor (bvxor a b) (bvxor (bvsub a b) a)))
  313|      0|  // )
  314|      0|  //
  315|      0|  // (declare-fun a () (_ BitVec 32))
  316|      0|  // (declare-fun b () (_ BitVec 32))
  317|      0|  //
  318|      0|  // (assert (not (= (= #x00000001 (bvlshr (lt a b) #x0000001f)) (bvult a b))))
  319|      0|  // (check-sat)
  320|      0|  // (get-model)
  321|      0|  return constant_time_msb_w(a ^ ((a ^ b) | ((a - b) ^ a)));
  322|      0|}
  323|       |
  324|       |// constant_time_lt_8 acts like `constant_time_lt_w` but returns an 8-bit
  325|       |// mask.
  326|      0|inline uint8_t constant_time_lt_8(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  327|      0|  return (uint8_t)(constant_time_lt_w(a, b));
  328|      0|}
  329|       |
  330|       |// constant_time_ge_w returns 0xff..f if a >= b and 0 otherwise.
  331|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_ge_w(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  332|      0|  return ~constant_time_lt_w(a, b);
  333|      0|}
  334|       |
  335|       |// constant_time_ge_8 acts like `constant_time_ge_w` but returns an 8-bit
  336|       |// mask.
  337|      0|inline uint8_t constant_time_ge_8(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  338|      0|  return (uint8_t)(constant_time_ge_w(a, b));
  339|      0|}
  340|       |
  341|       |// constant_time_is_zero returns 0xff..f if a == 0 and 0 otherwise.
  342|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_is_zero_w(crypto_word_t a) {
  343|       |  // Here is an SMT-LIB verification of this formula:
  344|       |  //
  345|       |  // (define-fun is_zero ((a (_ BitVec 32))) (_ BitVec 32)
  346|       |  //   (bvand (bvnot a) (bvsub a #x00000001))
  347|       |  // )
  348|       |  //
  349|       |  // (declare-fun a () (_ BitVec 32))
  350|       |  //
  351|       |  // (assert (not (= (= #x00000001 (bvlshr (is_zero a) #x0000001f)) (= a
  352|       |  // #x00000000)))) (check-sat) (get-model)
  353|      0|  return constant_time_msb_w(~a & (a - 1));
  354|      0|}
  355|       |
  356|       |// constant_time_is_zero_8 acts like `constant_time_is_zero_w` but returns an
  357|       |// 8-bit mask.
  358|      0|inline uint8_t constant_time_is_zero_8(crypto_word_t a) {
  359|      0|  return (uint8_t)(constant_time_is_zero_w(a));
  360|      0|}
  361|       |
  362|       |// constant_time_eq_w returns 0xff..f if a == b and 0 otherwise.
  363|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_eq_w(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  364|      0|  return constant_time_is_zero_w(a ^ b);
  365|      0|}
  366|       |
  367|       |// constant_time_eq_8 acts like `constant_time_eq_w` but returns an 8-bit
  368|       |// mask.
  369|      0|inline uint8_t constant_time_eq_8(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
  370|      0|  return (uint8_t)(constant_time_eq_w(a, b));
  371|      0|}
  372|       |
  373|       |// constant_time_eq_int acts like `constant_time_eq_w` but works on int
  374|       |// values.
  375|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_eq_int(int a, int b) {
  376|      0|  return constant_time_eq_w((crypto_word_t)(a), (crypto_word_t)(b));
  377|      0|}
  378|       |
  379|       |// constant_time_eq_int_8 acts like `constant_time_eq_int` but returns an 8-bit
  380|       |// mask.
  381|      0|inline uint8_t constant_time_eq_int_8(int a, int b) {
  382|      0|  return constant_time_eq_8((crypto_word_t)(a), (crypto_word_t)(b));
  383|      0|}
  384|       |
  385|       |// constant_time_select_w returns (mask & a) | (~mask & b). When `mask` is all
  386|       |// 1s or all 0s (as returned by the methods above), the select methods return
  387|       |// either `a` (if `mask` is nonzero) or `b` (if `mask` is zero).
  388|       |inline crypto_word_t constant_time_select_w(crypto_word_t mask, crypto_word_t a,
  389|      0|                                            crypto_word_t b) {
  390|      0|  // Clang recognizes this pattern as a select. While it usually transforms it
  391|      0|  // to a cmov, it sometimes further transforms it into a branch, which we do
  392|      0|  // not want.
  393|      0|  //
  394|      0|  // Hiding the value of the mask from the compiler evades this transformation.
  395|      0|  mask = value_barrier_w(mask);
  396|      0|  return (mask & a) | (~mask & b);
  397|      0|}
  398|       |
  399|       |// constant_time_select_8 acts like `constant_time_select` but operates on
  400|       |// 8-bit values.
  401|       |inline uint8_t constant_time_select_8(crypto_word_t mask, uint8_t a,
  402|      0|                                      uint8_t b) {
  403|      0|  // `mask` is a word instead of `uint8_t` to avoid materializing 0x000..0MM
  404|      0|  // Making both `mask` and its value barrier `uint8_t` would allow the compiler
  405|      0|  // to materialize 0x????..?MM instead, but only clang is that clever.
  406|      0|  // However, vectorization of bitwise operations seems to work better on
  407|      0|  // `uint8_t` than a mix of `uint64_t` and `uint8_t`, so `m` is cast to
  408|      0|  // `uint8_t` after the value barrier but before the bitwise operations.
  409|      0|  uint8_t m = value_barrier_w(mask);
  410|      0|  return (m & a) | (~m & b);
  411|      0|}
  412|       |
  413|       |// constant_time_select_int acts like `constant_time_select` but operates on
  414|       |// ints.
  415|      0|inline int constant_time_select_int(crypto_word_t mask, int a, int b) {
  416|      0|  return static_cast<int>(constant_time_select_w(
  417|      0|      mask, static_cast<crypto_word_t>(a), static_cast<crypto_word_t>(b)));
  418|      0|}
  419|       |
  420|       |// constant_time_select_32 acts like `constant_time_select` but operates on
  421|       |// 32-bit values.
  422|       |inline uint32_t constant_time_select_32(crypto_word_t mask, uint32_t a,
  423|      0|                                        uint32_t b) {
  424|      0|  return static_cast<uint32_t>(
  425|      0|      constant_time_select_w(mask, crypto_word_t{a}, crypto_word_t{b}));
  426|      0|}
  427|       |
  428|       |// constant_time_conditional_memcpy copies `n` bytes from `src` to `dst` if
  429|       |// `mask` is 0xff..ff and does nothing if `mask` is 0. The `n`-byte memory
  430|       |// ranges at `dst` and `src` must not overlap, as when calling `memcpy`.
  431|       |inline void constant_time_conditional_memcpy(void *dst, const void *src,
  432|       |                                             const size_t n,
  433|      0|                                             const crypto_word_t mask) {
  434|      0|  assert(!buffers_alias(dst, n, src, n));
  435|      0|  uint8_t *out = (uint8_t *)dst;
  436|      0|  const uint8_t *in = (const uint8_t *)src;
  437|      0|  for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
  438|      0|    out[i] = constant_time_select_8(mask, in[i], out[i]);
  439|      0|  }
  440|      0|}
  441|       |
  442|       |// constant_time_conditional_memxor xors `n` bytes from `src` to `dst` if
  443|       |// `mask` is 0xff..ff and does nothing if `mask` is 0. The `n`-byte memory
  444|       |// ranges at `dst` and `src` must not overlap, as when calling `memcpy`.
  445|       |inline void constant_time_conditional_memxor(void *dst, const void *src,
  446|       |                                             size_t n,
  447|      0|                                             const crypto_word_t mask) {
  448|      0|  assert(!buffers_alias(dst, n, src, n));
  449|      0|  uint8_t *out = (uint8_t *)dst;
  450|      0|  const uint8_t *in = (const uint8_t *)src;
  451|      0|#if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
  452|       |  // gcc 13.2.0 doesn't automatically vectorize this loop regardless of barrier
  453|       |  // clang 16.0.6 vectorizes if alias analysis finds src and dst are disjoint,
  454|       |  // but does not conclude that from the !buffers_alias assert.
  455|      0|  typedef uint8_t v32u8 __attribute__((vector_size(32), aligned(1), may_alias));
  456|      0|  size_t n_vec = n & ~(size_t)31;
  457|      0|  v32u8 masks = ((uint8_t)mask - (v32u8){});  // broadcast
  458|      0|  for (size_t i = 0; i < n_vec; i += 32) {
  459|      0|    *(v32u8 *)&out[i] ^= masks & *(v32u8 *)&in[i];
  460|      0|  }
  461|      0|  in += n_vec;
  462|      0|  out += n_vec;
  463|      0|  n -= n_vec;
  464|      0|#endif
  465|      0|  for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
  466|      0|    out[i] ^= value_barrier_w(mask) & in[i];
  467|      0|  }
  468|      0|}
  469|       |
  470|       |#if defined(BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION)
  471|       |
  472|       |// CONSTTIME_SECRET takes a pointer and a number of bytes and marks that region
  473|       |// of memory as secret. Secret data is tracked as it flows to registers and
  474|       |// other parts of a memory. If secret data is used as a condition for a branch,
  475|       |// or as a memory index, it will trigger warnings in valgrind.
  476|       |#define CONSTTIME_SECRET(ptr, len) VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_UNDEFINED(ptr, len)
  477|       |
  478|       |// CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY takes a pointer and a number of bytes and marks that
  479|       |// region of memory as public. Public data is not subject to constant-time
  480|       |// rules.
  481|       |#define CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(ptr, len) VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_DEFINED(ptr, len)
  482|       |
  483|       |#else
  484|       |
  485|       |// Just disable unused warnings for those.
  486|       |#define CONSTTIME_SECRET(ptr, len) \
  487|       |  do {                             \
  488|       |    (void)(ptr);                   \
  489|       |    (void)(len);                   \
  490|       |  } while (false)
  491|       |#define CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(ptr, len) \
  492|      0|  do {                                 \
  493|      0|    (void)(ptr);                       \
  494|      0|    (void)(len);                       \
  495|      0|  } while (false)
  496|       |
  497|       |#endif  // BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION
  498|       |
  499|      0|inline crypto_word_t constant_time_declassify_w(crypto_word_t v) {
  500|      0|  // Return `v` through a value barrier to be safe. Valgrind-based constant-time
  501|      0|  // validation is partly to check the compiler has not undone any constant-time
  502|      0|  // work. Any place `BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION` influences
  503|      0|  // optimizations, this validation is inaccurate.
  504|      0|  //
  505|      0|  // However, by sending pointers through valgrind, we likely inhibit escape
  506|      0|  // analysis. On local variables, particularly booleans, we likely
  507|      0|  // significantly impact optimizations.
  508|      0|  //
  509|      0|  // Thus, to be safe, stick a value barrier, in hopes of comparably inhibiting
  510|      0|  // compiler analysis.
  511|      0|  CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(&v, sizeof(v));
  512|      0|  return value_barrier_w(v);
  513|      0|}
  514|       |
  515|      0|inline int constant_time_declassify_int(int v) {
  516|      0|  static_assert(sizeof(uint32_t) == sizeof(int),
  517|      0|                "int is not the same size as uint32_t");
  518|       |  // See comment above.
  519|      0|  CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(&v, sizeof(v));
  520|      0|  return value_barrier_u32(v);
  521|      0|}
  522|       |
  523|       |// declassify_assert behaves like `assert` but declassifies the result of
  524|       |// evaluating `expr`. This allows the assertion to branch on the (presumably
  525|       |// public) result, but still ensures that values leading up to the computation
  526|       |// were secret.
  527|      0|#define declassify_assert(expr) assert(constant_time_declassify_int(expr))
  528|       |
  529|       |
  530|       |// Thread-safe initialisation.
  531|       |
  532|       |#if !defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
  533|       |typedef uint32_t CRYPTO_once_t;
  534|       |#define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT 0
  535|       |#elif defined(OPENSSL_WINDOWS_THREADS)
  536|       |typedef INIT_ONCE CRYPTO_once_t;
  537|       |#define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT INIT_ONCE_STATIC_INIT
  538|       |#elif defined(OPENSSL_PTHREADS)
  539|       |typedef pthread_once_t CRYPTO_once_t;
  540|       |#define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT PTHREAD_ONCE_INIT
  541|       |#else
  542|       |#error "Unknown threading library"
  543|       |#endif
  544|       |
  545|       |// CRYPTO_once calls `init` exactly once per process. This is thread-safe: if
  546|       |// concurrent threads call `CRYPTO_once` with the same `CRYPTO_once_t` argument
  547|       |// then they will block until `init` completes, but `init` will have only been
  548|       |// called once.
  549|       |//
  550|       |// The `once` argument must be a `CRYPTO_once_t` that has been initialised with
  551|       |// the value `CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT`.
  552|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void CRYPTO_once(CRYPTO_once_t *once, void (*init)());
  553|       |
  554|       |
  555|       |// Atomics.
  556|       |//
  557|       |// This is a thin wrapper over std::atomic because some embedded platforms do
  558|       |// not support threads and don't provide a trivial std::atomic implementation.
  559|       |// For now, this does not wrap std::memory_order. If we ever use non-default
  560|       |// std::memory_order, we will need to wrap these too, or fix the embedded
  561|       |// platforms to provide a no-op std::atomic. See https://crbug.com/442112336.
  562|       |
  563|       |#if defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
  564|       |template <typename T>
  565|       |using Atomic = std::atomic<T>;
  566|       |#else
  567|       |template <typename T>
  568|       |class Atomic {
  569|       | public:
  570|       |  static_assert(std::is_integral_v<T> || std::is_pointer_v<T>);
  571|       |
  572|       |  Atomic() = default;
  573|       |  constexpr Atomic(T value) : value_(value) {}
  574|       |  Atomic(const Atomic &) = delete;
  575|       |  Atomic &operator=(const Atomic &) = delete;
  576|       |  T operator=(T value) {
  577|       |    value_ = value;
  578|       |    return value_;
  579|       |  }
  580|       |
  581|       |  T load() const { return value_; }
  582|       |  void store(T desired) { value_ = desired; }
  583|       |
  584|       |  bool compare_exchange_strong(T &expected, T desired) {
  585|       |    if (value_ != expected) {
  586|       |      expected = value_;
  587|       |      return false;
  588|       |    }
  589|       |    value_ = desired;
  590|       |    return true;
  591|       |  }
  592|       |  bool compare_exchange_weak(T &expected, T desired) {
  593|       |    return compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
  594|       |  }
  595|       |
  596|       |  T exchange(T desired) { return std::exchange(value_, desired); }
  597|       |
  598|       | private:
  599|       |  T value_;
  600|       |};
  601|       |#endif
  602|       |
  603|       |
  604|       |// Reference counting.
  605|       |
  606|       |// CRYPTO_REFCOUNT_MAX is the value at which the reference count saturates.
  607|       |#define CRYPTO_REFCOUNT_MAX 0xffffffff
  608|       |
  609|       |using CRYPTO_refcount_t = Atomic<uint32_t>;
  610|       |
  611|       |// CRYPTO_refcount_inc atomically increments the value at `*count` unless the
  612|       |// value would overflow. It's safe for multiple threads to concurrently call
  613|       |// this or `CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero` on the same
  614|       |// `CRYPTO_refcount_t`.
  615|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void CRYPTO_refcount_inc(CRYPTO_refcount_t *count);
  616|       |
  617|       |// CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero tests the value at `*count`:
  618|       |//   if it's zero, it crashes the address space.
  619|       |//   if it's the maximum value, it returns zero.
  620|       |//   otherwise, it atomically decrements it and returns one iff the resulting
  621|       |//       value is zero.
  622|       |//
  623|       |// It's safe for multiple threads to concurrently call this or
  624|       |// `CRYPTO_refcount_inc` on the same `CRYPTO_refcount_t`.
  625|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero(CRYPTO_refcount_t *count);
  626|       |
  627|       |
  628|       |// Locks.
  629|       |
  630|       |// A Mutex is a read/write lock. It can be constant-initialized, but has a
  631|       |// destructor. To allocate a global one, use StaticMutex, which skips the
  632|       |// destructor.
  633|       |class OPENSSL_EXPORT StaticMutex {
  634|       | public:
  635|       |  constexpr StaticMutex() = default;
  636|       |  StaticMutex(const StaticMutex &) = delete;
  637|       |  StaticMutex &operator=(const StaticMutex &) = delete;
  638|       |
  639|       |  // LockRead locks the mutex such that other threads may also have a read lock,
  640|       |  // but none may have a write lock.
  641|       |  void LockRead();
  642|       |  // UnlockRead releases a read lock.
  643|       |  void UnlockRead();
  644|       |
  645|       |  // LockWrite locks the mutex such that no other thread has any type of lock on
  646|       |  // it.
  647|       |  void LockWrite();
  648|       |  // UnlockWrite releases a write lock.
  649|       |  void UnlockWrite();
  650|       |
  651|       |protected:
  652|       |#if !defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
  653|       |  // Nothing.
  654|       |#elif defined(OPENSSL_WINDOWS_THREADS)
  655|       |  SRWLOCK lock_ = SRWLOCK_INIT;
  656|       |#elif defined(OPENSSL_PTHREADS)
  657|       |  pthread_rwlock_t lock_ = PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER;
  658|       |#else
  659|       |#error "Unknown threading library"
  660|       |#endif
  661|       |};
  662|       |
  663|       |class OPENSSL_EXPORT Mutex  : public StaticMutex {
  664|       | public:
  665|       |  constexpr Mutex() = default;
  666|       |  ~Mutex();
  667|       |};
  668|       |
  669|       |namespace internal {
  670|       |
  671|       |// MutexLockBase is a RAII helper for Mutex locking.
  672|       |template <void (StaticMutex::*LockMethod)(),
  673|       |          void (StaticMutex::*ReleaseMethod)()>
  674|       |class MutexLockBase {
  675|       | public:
  676|       |  explicit MutexLockBase(StaticMutex *mu) : mu_(mu) {
  677|       |    assert(mu_ != nullptr);
  678|       |    (mu_->*LockMethod)();
  679|       |  }
  680|       |  ~MutexLockBase() { (mu_->*ReleaseMethod)(); }
  681|       |  MutexLockBase(const MutexLockBase &) = delete;
  682|       |  MutexLockBase &operator=(const MutexLockBase &) = delete;
  683|       |
  684|       | private:
  685|       |  StaticMutex *const mu_;
  686|       |};
  687|       |
  688|       |}  // namespace internal
  689|       |
  690|       |using MutexWriteLock =
  691|       |    internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::LockWrite, &StaticMutex::UnlockWrite>;
  692|       |using MutexReadLock =
  693|       |    internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::LockRead, &StaticMutex::UnlockRead>;
  694|       |using MutexWriteUnlock =
  695|       |    internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::UnlockWrite, &StaticMutex::LockWrite>;
  696|       |using MutexReadUnlock =
  697|       |    internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::UnlockRead, &StaticMutex::LockRead>;
  698|       |
  699|       |
  700|       |// Thread local storage.
  701|       |
  702|       |// thread_local_data_t enumerates the types of thread-local data that can be
  703|       |// stored.
  704|       |typedef enum {
  705|       |  OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_ERR = 0,
  706|       |  OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_RAND,
  707|       |  OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_FIPS_COUNTERS,
  708|       |  OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_FIPS_SERVICE_INDICATOR_STATE,
  709|       |  OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_TEST,
  710|       |  NUM_OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCALS,
  711|       |} thread_local_data_t;
  712|       |
  713|       |// thread_local_destructor_t is the type of a destructor function that will be
  714|       |// called when a thread exits and its thread-local storage needs to be freed.
  715|       |typedef void (*thread_local_destructor_t)(void *);
  716|       |
  717|       |// CRYPTO_get_thread_local gets the pointer value that is stored for the
  718|       |// current thread for the given index, or NULL if none has been set.
  719|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void *CRYPTO_get_thread_local(thread_local_data_t value);
  720|       |
  721|       |// CRYPTO_set_thread_local sets a pointer value for the current thread at the
  722|       |// given index. This function should only be called once per thread for a given
  723|       |// `index`: rather than update the pointer value itself, update the data that
  724|       |// is pointed to.
  725|       |//
  726|       |// The destructor function will be called when a thread exits to free this
  727|       |// thread-local data. All calls to `CRYPTO_set_thread_local` with the same
  728|       |// `index` should have the same `destructor` argument. The destructor may be
  729|       |// called with a NULL argument if a thread that never set a thread-local
  730|       |// pointer for `index`, exits. The destructor may be called concurrently with
  731|       |// different arguments.
  732|       |//
  733|       |// This function returns one on success or zero on error. If it returns zero
  734|       |// then `destructor` has been called with `value` already.
  735|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int CRYPTO_set_thread_local(
  736|       |    thread_local_data_t index, void *value,
  737|       |    thread_local_destructor_t destructor);

  744|       |struct crypto_ex_data_st {
  745|       |  STACK_OF(void) *sk;
  746|       |} /* CRYPTO_EX_DATA */;

  749|       |
  750|       |struct ExDataFuncs;
  751|       |
  752|       |// ExDataClass tracks the ex_indices registered for a type which
  753|       |// supports ex_data. It should defined as a static global within the module
  754|       |// which defines that type.
  755|       |struct ExDataClass {
  756|       |  explicit constexpr ExDataClass(bool with_app_data = false)
  757|      0|      : num_reserved(with_app_data ? 1 : 0) {}
  758|       |
  759|       |  StaticMutex lock;
  760|       |  // funcs is a linked list of `ExDataFuncs` structures. It may be traversed
  761|       |  // without serialization only up to `num_funcs`. last points to the final
  762|       |  // entry of `funcs`, or nullptr if empty.
  763|       |  ExDataFuncs *funcs = nullptr, *last = nullptr;
  764|       |  // num_funcs is the number of entries in `funcs`.
  765|       |  Atomic<uint32_t> num_funcs = 0;
  766|       |  // num_reserved is one if the ex_data index zero is reserved for legacy
  767|       |  // `TYPE_get_app_data` functions.
  768|       |  uint8_t num_reserved = 0;
  769|       |};
  770|       |

  795|       |// Endianness conversions.
  796|       |
  797|       |#if defined(__GNUC__) && __GNUC__ >= 2
  798|      0|inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return __builtin_bswap16(x); }
  799|       |
  800|  4.17M|inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) { return __builtin_bswap32(x); }
  801|       |
  802|      0|inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) { return __builtin_bswap64(x); }
  803|       |#elif defined(_MSC_VER)
  804|       |#pragma intrinsic(_byteswap_uint64, _byteswap_ulong, _byteswap_ushort)
  805|       |inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return _byteswap_ushort(x); }
  806|       |
  807|       |inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) { return _byteswap_ulong(x); }
  808|       |
  809|       |inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) { return _byteswap_uint64(x); }
  810|       |#else
  811|       |inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return (x >> 8) | (x << 8); }
  812|       |
  813|       |inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) {
  814|       |  x = (x >> 16) | (x << 16);
  815|       |  x = ((x & 0xff00ff00) >> 8) | ((x & 0x00ff00ff) << 8);
  816|       |  return x;
  817|       |}
  818|       |
  819|       |inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) {
  820|       |  return CRYPTO_bswap4(x >> 32) | (((uint64_t)CRYPTO_bswap4(x)) << 32);
  821|       |}
  822|       |#endif
  823|       |
  824|       |
  825|       |// Language bug workarounds.
  826|       |//
  827|       |// Most C standard library functions are undefined if passed NULL, even when the
  828|       |// corresponding length is zero. This gives them (and, in turn, all functions
  829|       |// which call them) surprising behavior on empty arrays. Some compilers will
  830|       |// miscompile code due to this rule. See also
  831|       |// https://www.imperialviolet.org/2016/06/26/nonnull.html
  832|       |//
  833|       |// These wrapper functions behave the same as the corresponding C standard
  834|       |// functions, but behave as expected when passed NULL if the length is zero.
  835|       |//
  836|       |// Note `OPENSSL_memcmp` is a different function from `CRYPTO_memcmp`.
  837|       |
  838|       |// C++ defines `memchr` as a const-correct overload.
  839|      0|inline const void *OPENSSL_memchr(const void *s, int c, size_t n) {
  840|      0|  if (n == 0) {
  841|      0|    return nullptr;
  842|      0|  }
  843|      0|
  844|      0|  return memchr(s, c, n);
  845|      0|}
  846|       |
  847|      0|inline void *OPENSSL_memchr(void *s, int c, size_t n) {
  848|      0|  if (n == 0) {
  849|      0|    return nullptr;
  850|      0|  }
  851|      0|
  852|      0|  return memchr(s, c, n);
  853|      0|}
  854|       |
  855|      0|inline int OPENSSL_memcmp(const void *s1, const void *s2, size_t n) {
  856|      0|  if (n == 0) {
  857|      0|    return 0;
  858|      0|  }
  859|      0|
  860|      0|  return memcmp(s1, s2, n);
  861|      0|}
  862|       |
  863|  4.33M|inline void *OPENSSL_memcpy(void *dst, const void *src, size_t n) {
  864|  4.33M|  if (n == 0) {
  865|      0|    return dst;
  866|      0|  }
  867|       |
  868|  4.33M|  return memcpy(dst, src, n);
  869|  4.33M|}
  870|       |
  871|      0|inline void *OPENSSL_memmove(void *dst, const void *src, size_t n) {
  872|      0|  if (n == 0) {
  873|      0|    return dst;
  874|      0|  }
  875|       |
  876|      0|  return memmove(dst, src, n);
  877|      0|}
  878|       |
  879|   481k|inline void *OPENSSL_memset(void *dst, int c, size_t n) {
  880|   481k|  if (n == 0) {
  881|      0|    return dst;
  882|      0|  }

  980|       |// Bit rotation functions.
  981|       |//
  982|       |// Note these functions use `(-shift) & 31`, etc., because shifting by the bit
  983|       |// width is undefined. Both Clang and GCC recognize this pattern as a rotation,
  984|       |// but MSVC does not. Instead, we call MSVC's built-in functions.
  985|       |
  986|      0|inline uint32_t CRYPTO_rotl_u32(uint32_t value, int shift) {
  987|      0|#if defined(_MSC_VER)
  988|      0|  return _rotl(value, shift);
  989|      0|#else
  990|      0|  return (value << shift) | (value >> ((-shift) & 31));
  991|      0|#endif
  992|      0|}
  993|       |
  994|  92.4M|inline uint32_t CRYPTO_rotr_u32(uint32_t value, int shift) {
  995|       |#if defined(_MSC_VER)
  996|       |  return _rotr(value, shift);
  997|       |#else
  998|  92.4M|  return (value >> shift) | (value << ((-shift) & 31));
  999|  92.4M|#endif
 1000|  92.4M|}
 1001|       |
 1002|      0|inline uint64_t CRYPTO_rotl_u64(uint64_t value, int shift) {
 1003|      0|#if defined(_MSC_VER)
 1004|      0|  return _rotl64(value, shift);
 1005|      0|#else
 1006|      0|  return (value << shift) | (value >> ((-shift) & 63));
 1007|      0|#endif
 1008|      0|}
 1009|       |
 1010|      0|inline uint64_t CRYPTO_rotr_u64(uint64_t value, int shift) {

 1103|       |
 1104|       |// Runtime CPU feature support
 1105|       |
 1106|       |#if defined(OPENSSL_X86) || defined(OPENSSL_X86_64)
 1107|       |// OPENSSL_ia32cap_P contains the Intel CPUID bits when running on an x86 or
 1108|       |// x86-64 system.
 1109|       |//
 1110|       |//   Index 0:
 1111|       |//     EDX for CPUID where EAX = 1
 1112|       |//     Bit 30 is used to indicate an Intel CPU
 1113|       |//   Index 1:
 1114|       |//     ECX for CPUID where EAX = 1
 1115|       |//   Index 2:
 1116|       |//     EBX for CPUID where EAX = 7, ECX = 0
 1117|       |//     Bit 14 (for removed feature MPX) is used to indicate a preference for ymm
 1118|       |//       registers over zmm even when zmm registers are supported
 1119|       |//   Index 3:
 1120|       |//     ECX for CPUID where EAX = 7, ECX = 0
 1121|       |//
 1122|       |// Note: the CPUID bits are pre-adjusted for the OSXSAVE bit and the XMM, YMM,
 1123|       |// and AVX512 bits in XCR0, so it is not necessary to check those. (WARNING: See
 1124|       |// caveats in cpu_intel.c.)
 1125|       |//
 1126|       |// This symbol should only be accessed with `OPENSSL_get_ia32cap`.
 1127|       |extern uint32_t OPENSSL_ia32cap_P[4];
 1128|       |
 1129|       |// OPENSSL_get_ia32cap initializes the library if needed and returns the `idx`th
 1130|       |// entry of `OPENSSL_ia32cap_P`. It is marked as a const function so duplicate
 1131|       |// calls can be merged by the compiler, at least when indices match.
 1132|       |OPENSSL_ATTR_CONST uint32_t OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(int idx);
 1133|       |
 1134|       |// OPENSSL_adjust_ia32cap adjusts `cap`, which should contain
 1135|       |// `OPENSSL_ia32cap_P`, based on the environment variable value in `env`. This
 1136|       |// function is exposed for unit tests.
 1137|       |void OPENSSL_adjust_ia32cap(uint32_t cap[4], const char *env);
 1138|       |
 1139|       |// See Intel manual, volume 2A, table 3-11.
 1140|       |
 1141|      0|inline int CRYPTO_is_intel_cpu() {
 1142|      0|  // The reserved bit 30 is used to indicate an Intel CPU.
 1143|      0|  return (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(0) & (1u << 30)) != 0;
 1144|      0|}
 1145|       |
 1146|       |// See Intel manual, volume 2A, table 3-10.
 1147|       |

 1150|      0|  return 1;

 1248|       |
 1249|       |// CRYPTO_cpu_perf_is_like_silvermont returns one if, based on a heuristic, the
 1250|       |// CPU has Silvermont-like performance characteristics. It is often faster to
 1251|       |// run different codepaths on these CPUs than the available instructions would
 1252|       |// otherwise select. See chacha-x86_64.pl.
 1253|       |//
 1254|       |// Bonnell, Silvermont's predecessor in the Atom lineup, will also be matched by
 1255|       |// this. Goldmont (Silvermont's successor in the Atom lineup) added XSAVE so it
 1256|       |// isn't matched by this. Various sources indicate AMD first implemented MOVBE
 1257|       |// and XSAVE at the same time in Jaguar, so it seems like AMD chips will not be
 1258|       |// matched by this. That seems to be the case for other x86(-64) CPUs.
 1259|      0|inline int CRYPTO_cpu_perf_is_like_silvermont() {
 1260|      0|  // WARNING: This MUST NOT be used to guard the execution of the XSAVE
 1261|      0|  // instruction. This is the "hardware supports XSAVE" bit, not the OSXSAVE bit
 1262|      0|  // that indicates whether we can safely execute XSAVE. This bit may be set
 1263|      0|  // even when XSAVE is disabled (by the operating system). See how the users of
 1264|      0|  // this bit use it.
 1265|      0|  //
 1266|      0|  // Historically, the XSAVE bit was artificially cleared on Knights Landing
 1267|      0|  // and Knights Mill chips, but as Intel has removed all support from GCC,
 1268|      0|  // LLVM, and SDE, we assume they are no longer worth special-casing.
 1269|      0|  int hardware_supports_xsave = (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(1) & (1u << 26)) != 0;
 1270|      0|  return !hardware_supports_xsave && CRYPTO_is_MOVBE_capable();
 1271|      0|}
 1272|       |
 1273|      0|inline int CRYPTO_is_AVX512BW_capable() {
 1274|      0|#if defined(__AVX512BW__)
 1275|      0|  return 1;
 1276|      0|#else
 1277|      0|  return (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(2) & (1u << 30)) != 0;
 1278|      0|#endif
 1279|      0|}
 1280|       |
 1281|      0|inline int CRYPTO_is_AVX512VL_capable() {
 1282|      0|#if defined(__AVX512VL__)
 1283|      0|  return 1;
 1284|      0|#else
 1285|      0|  return (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(2) & (1u << 31)) != 0;
 1286|      0|#endif
 1287|      0|}
 1288|       |
 1289|       |// CRYPTO_cpu_avoid_zmm_registers returns 1 if zmm registers (512-bit vectors)
 1290|       |// should not be used even if the CPU supports them.
 1291|       |//
 1292|       |// Note that this reuses the bit for the removed MPX feature.
 1293|      0|inline int CRYPTO_cpu_avoid_zmm_registers() {
 1294|      0|  return (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(2) & (1u << 14)) != 0;
 1295|      0|}
 1296|       |

 1498|      0|}
 1499|       |
 1500|       |inline uint32_t CRYPTO_addc_u32(uint32_t x, uint32_t y, uint32_t carry,
 1501|      0|                                uint32_t *out_carry) {
 1502|      0|  return CRYPTO_addc_impl(x, y, carry, out_carry);
 1503|      0|}
 1504|       |
 1505|       |inline uint64_t CRYPTO_addc_u64(uint64_t x, uint64_t y, uint64_t carry,
 1506|      0|                                uint64_t *out_carry) {
 1507|      0|  return CRYPTO_addc_impl(x, y, carry, out_carry);
 1508|      0|}
 1509|       |
 1510|       |#else
 1511|       |
 1512|       |inline uint32_t CRYPTO_addc_u32(uint32_t x, uint32_t y, uint32_t carry,
 1513|       |                                uint32_t *out_carry) {
 1514|       |  declassify_assert(carry <= 1);
 1515|       |#if defined(_M_IX86)
 1516|       |  uint32_t sum = 0;
 1517|       |  *out_carry = _addcarry_u32(carry, x, y, &sum);
 1518|       |  return sum;
 1519|       |#else
 1520|       |  uint64_t ret = carry;
 1521|       |  ret += (uint64_t)x + y;
 1522|       |  *out_carry = (uint32_t)(ret >> 32);
 1523|       |  return (uint32_t)ret;
 1524|       |#endif
 1525|       |}
 1526|       |
 1527|       |inline uint64_t CRYPTO_addc_u64(uint64_t x, uint64_t y, uint64_t carry,
 1528|       |                                uint64_t *out_carry) {
 1529|       |  declassify_assert(carry <= 1);
 1530|       |#if defined(_M_X64)
 1531|       |  uint64_t sum = 0;
 1532|       |  *out_carry = _addcarry_u64(carry, x, y, &sum);
 1533|       |  return sum;
 1534|       |#elif defined(BORINGSSL_HAS_UINT128)
 1535|       |  uint128_t ret = carry;
 1536|       |  ret += (uint128_t)x + y;
 1537|       |  *out_carry = (uint64_t)(ret >> 64);
 1538|       |  return (uint64_t)ret;
 1539|       |#else
 1540|       |  x += carry;
 1541|       |  carry = x < carry;
 1542|       |  uint64_t ret = x + y;
 1543|       |  carry += ret < x;
 1544|       |  *out_carry = carry;
 1545|       |  return ret;
 1546|       |#endif
 1547|       |}
 1548|       |#endif
 1549|       |

 1582|      0|}

 1586|       |inline uint32_t CRYPTO_subc_u32(uint32_t x, uint32_t y, uint32_t borrow,
 1587|       |                                uint32_t *out_borrow) {
 1588|       |  declassify_assert(borrow <= 1);
 1589|       |#if defined(_M_IX86)
 1590|       |  uint32_t diff = 0;
 1591|       |  *out_borrow = _subborrow_u32(borrow, x, y, &diff);
 1592|       |  return diff;
 1593|       |#else
 1594|       |  uint32_t ret = x - y - borrow;
 1595|       |  *out_borrow = (x < y) | ((x == y) & borrow);
 1596|       |  return ret;
 1597|       |#endif
 1598|       |}
 1599|       |
 1600|       |inline uint64_t CRYPTO_subc_u64(uint64_t x, uint64_t y, uint64_t borrow,
 1601|       |                                uint64_t *out_borrow) {
 1602|       |  declassify_assert(borrow <= 1);
 1603|       |#if defined(_M_X64)
 1604|       |  uint64_t diff = 0;
 1605|       |  *out_borrow = _subborrow_u64(borrow, x, y, &diff);
 1606|       |  return diff;
 1607|       |#else
 1608|       |  uint64_t ret = x - y - borrow;
 1609|       |  *out_borrow = (x < y) | ((x == y) & borrow);
 1610|       |  return ret;
 1611|       |#endif
 1612|       |}
 1613|       |#endif

 1624|       |// Cleanup implements a custom scope guard, when the cleanup logic does not fit
 1625|       |// in a destructor. Usage:
 1626|       |//
 1627|       |//     bssl::Cleanup cleanup = [&] { SomeCleanupWork(local_var); };
 1628|       |template <typename F>
 1629|       |class Cleanup {
 1630|       | public:
 1631|       |  static_assert(std::is_invocable_v<F>);
 1632|       |  static_assert(std::is_same_v<std::invoke_result_t<F>, void>);
 1633|       |
 1634|       |  Cleanup(F func) : func_(func) {}
 1635|       |  Cleanup(const Cleanup &) = delete;
 1636|       |  Cleanup &operator=(const Cleanup &) = delete;
 1637|       |  ~Cleanup() { func_(); }
 1638|       |
 1639|       | private:
 1640|       |  F func_;
 1641|       |};
 1642|       |template <typename F>
 1643|       |Cleanup(F func) -> Cleanup<F>;
 1644|       |
 1645|       |// DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT defines a public struct `public_name` with an
 1646|       |// implementation struct `impl_name`.
 1647|       |//
 1648|       |// To prevent accidents, the `public_name` struct will be neither constructable,
 1649|       |// nor copyable/movable, nor deletable.
 1650|       |//
 1651|       |// It must be used from inside the `bssl` namespace; however, `public_name` will
 1652|       |// be defined outside.
 1653|       |//
 1654|       |// Usage:
 1655|       |//
 1656|       |// DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT(public_st, PublicImpl)
 1657|       |//
 1658|       |// BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
 1659|       |//
 1660|       |// class PublicImpl : public public_st {
 1661|       |//  public:
 1662|       |//   PublicImpl();
 1663|       |//   ~PublicImpl();
 1664|       |//   void foo();
 1665|       |// };
 1666|       |//
 1667|       |// BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
 1668|       |//
 1669|       |// The implementation struct can be converted to the public struct implicitly;
 1670|       |// to convert the public struct to the implementation struct, call
 1671|       |// `FromOpaque` on it. It is explicitly allowed to call `FromOpaque` on a
 1672|       |// `nullptr`.
 1673|       |#define DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT(public_name, impl_name)                  \
 1674|       |  BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN                                                 \
 1675|       |  class impl_name;                                                     \
 1676|       |  BSSL_NAMESPACE_END                                                   \
 1677|       |                                                                       \
 1678|       |  /* This is unnamespaced but assumed to not create linker symbols. */ \
 1679|       |  struct public_name {                                                 \
 1680|       |    using ImplType = bssl::impl_name;                                  \
 1681|       |                                                                       \
 1682|       |   private:                                                            \
 1683|       |    public_name() = default;                                           \
 1684|       |    ~public_name() = default;                                          \
 1685|       |    public_name(const public_name &) = delete;                         \
 1686|       |    public_name &operator=(const public_name &) = delete;              \
 1687|       |                                                                       \
 1688|       |    friend class bssl::impl_name;                                      \
 1689|       |  };
 1690|       |
 1691|       |template <typename Public>
 1692|       |inline typename Public::ImplType *FromOpaque(Public *p) {
 1693|       |  return static_cast<typename Public::ImplType *>(p);
 1694|       |}
 1695|       |
 1696|       |template <typename Public>
 1697|       |inline const typename Public::ImplType *FromOpaque(const Public *p) {
 1698|       |  return static_cast<const typename Public::ImplType *>(p);
 1699|       |}
 1700|       |
 1701|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
 1702|       |
 1703|       |
 1704|       |#endif  // OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_INTERNAL_H

   59|      0|int SHA256_Final(uint8_t out[SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH], SHA256_CTX *sha) {
   60|       |  // TODO(bbe): This overflow check one of the few places a low-level hash
   61|       |  // 'final' function can fail. SHA-512 does not have a corresponding check.
   62|       |  // The BCM function is infallible and will abort if this is done incorrectly.
   63|       |  // we should verify nothing crashes with this removed and eliminate the 0
   64|       |  // return.
   65|      0|  if (sha->md_len > SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH) {
   66|      0|    return 0;
   67|      0|  }
   68|      0|  BCM_sha256_final(out, sha);
   69|      0|  return 1;
   70|      0|}
   71|       |
   72|       |uint8_t *SHA256(const uint8_t *data, size_t len,
   73|   160k|                uint8_t out[SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH]) {

  368|       |// BSSL_CHECK aborts if `condition` is not true.
  369|       |#define BSSL_CHECK(condition) \

  401|       |// Work around consumers including our headers under extern "C".
  402|       |extern "C++" {
  403|       |
  404|       |#include <memory>
  405|       |
  406|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
  407|       |
  408|       |namespace internal {
  409|       |
  410|       |// The Enable parameter is ignored and only exists so specializations can use
  411|       |// SFINAE.
  412|       |template <typename T, typename Enable = void>
  413|       |struct DeleterImpl {};
  414|       |
  415|       |struct Deleter {
  416|       |  template <typename T>
  417|       |  void operator()(T *ptr) {
  418|       |    // Rather than specialize Deleter for each type, we specialize
  419|       |    // DeleterImpl. This allows bssl::UniquePtr<T> to be used while only
  420|       |    // including base.h as long as the destructor is not emitted. This matches
  421|       |    // std::unique_ptr's behavior on forward-declared types.
  422|       |    //
  423|       |    // DeleterImpl itself is specialized in the corresponding module's header
  424|       |    // and must be included to release an object. If not included, the compiler
  425|       |    // will error that DeleterImpl<T> does not have a method Free.
  426|       |    DeleterImpl<T>::Free(ptr);
  427|       |  }
  428|       |};
  429|       |
  430|       |template <typename T, typename CleanupRet, void (*init)(T *),
  431|       |          CleanupRet (*cleanup)(T *)>
  432|       |class StackAllocated {
  433|       | public:
  434|       |  StackAllocated() { init(&ctx_); }
  435|       |  ~StackAllocated() { cleanup(&ctx_); }
  436|       |
  437|       |  StackAllocated(const StackAllocated &) = delete;
  438|       |  StackAllocated &operator=(const StackAllocated &) = delete;
  439|       |
  440|       |  T *get() { return &ctx_; }
  441|       |  const T *get() const { return &ctx_; }
  442|       |
  443|       |  T *operator->() { return &ctx_; }
  444|       |  const T *operator->() const { return &ctx_; }
  445|       |
  446|       |  void Reset() {
  447|       |    cleanup(&ctx_);
  448|       |    init(&ctx_);
  449|       |  }
  450|       |
  451|       | private:
  452|       |  T ctx_;
  453|       |};
  454|       |
  455|       |template <typename T, typename CleanupRet, void (*init)(T *),
  456|       |          CleanupRet (*cleanup)(T *), void (*move)(T *, T *)>
  457|       |class StackAllocatedMovable {
  458|       | public:
  459|       |  StackAllocatedMovable() { init(&ctx_); }
  460|       |  ~StackAllocatedMovable() { cleanup(&ctx_); }
  461|       |
  462|       |  StackAllocatedMovable(StackAllocatedMovable &&other) {
  463|       |    init(&ctx_);
  464|       |    move(&ctx_, &other.ctx_);
  465|       |  }
  466|       |  StackAllocatedMovable &operator=(StackAllocatedMovable &&other) {
  467|       |    move(&ctx_, &other.ctx_);
  468|       |    return *this;
  469|       |  }
  470|       |
  471|       |  T *get() { return &ctx_; }
  472|       |  const T *get() const { return &ctx_; }
  473|       |
  474|       |  T *operator->() { return &ctx_; }
  475|       |  const T *operator->() const { return &ctx_; }
  476|       |
  477|       |  void Reset() {
  478|       |    cleanup(&ctx_);
  479|       |    init(&ctx_);
  480|       |  }
  481|       |
  482|       | private:
  483|       |  T ctx_;
  484|       |};
  485|       |
  486|       |}  // namespace internal
  487|       |
  488|       |#define BORINGSSL_MAKE_DELETER(type, deleter)     \
  489|       |  namespace internal {                            \
  490|       |  template <>                                     \
  491|       |  struct DeleterImpl<type> {                      \
  492|      0|    static void Free(type *ptr) { deleter(ptr); } \
  ------------------
  | Unexecuted instantiation: _ZN4bssl8internal11DeleterImplI13spake2_ctx_stvE4FreeEPS2_
  ------------------
  | Unexecuted instantiation: _ZN4bssl8internal11DeleterImplIcvE4FreeEPc
  ------------------
  | Unexecuted instantiation: _ZN4bssl8internal11DeleterImplIhvE4FreeEPh
  ------------------
  493|       |  };                                              \
  494|       |  }
  495|       |
  496|       |// Holds ownership of heap-allocated BoringSSL structures. Sample usage:
  497|       |//   bssl::UniquePtr<RSA> rsa(RSA_new());
  498|       |//   bssl::UniquePtr<BIO> bio(BIO_new(BIO_s_mem()));
  499|       |template <typename T>
  500|       |using UniquePtr = std::unique_ptr<T, internal::Deleter>;
  501|       |
  502|       |#define BORINGSSL_MAKE_UP_REF(type, up_ref_func)             \
  503|       |  inline UniquePtr<type> UpRef(type *v) {                    \
  504|       |    if (v != nullptr) {                                      \
  505|       |      up_ref_func(v);                                        \
  506|       |    }                                                        \
  507|       |    return UniquePtr<type>(v);                               \
  508|       |  }                                                          \
  509|       |                                                             \
  510|       |  inline UniquePtr<type> UpRef(const UniquePtr<type> &ptr) { \
  511|       |    return UpRef(ptr.get());                                 \
  512|       |  }
  513|       |
  514|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
  515|       |
  516|       |}  // extern C++
  517|       |
  518|       |#endif  // !BORINGSSL_NO_CXX
  519|       |
  520|       |#endif  // OPENSSL_HEADER_BASE_H

    1|       |// Copyright 2015 The BoringSSL Authors
    2|       |//
    3|       |// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");

    5|       |// You may obtain a copy of the License at
    6|       |//

    8|       |//

   10|       |// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
   11|       |// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
   12|       |// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   13|       |// limitations under the License.
   14|       |
   15|       |#ifndef OPENSSL_HEADER_CURVE25519_H
   16|       |#define OPENSSL_HEADER_CURVE25519_H
   17|       |
   18|       |#include <openssl/base.h>   // IWYU pragma: export
   19|       |
   20|       |#if defined(__cplusplus)
   21|       |extern "C" {
   22|       |#endif
   23|       |
   24|       |
   25|       |// Curve25519.
   26|       |//
   27|       |// Curve25519 is an elliptic curve. See https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7748.
   28|       |
   29|       |
   30|       |// X25519.
   31|       |//
   32|       |// X25519 is the Diffie-Hellman primitive built from curve25519. It is
   33|       |// sometimes referred to as “curve25519”, but “X25519” is a more precise name.
   34|       |// See http://cr.yp.to/ecdh.html and https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7748.
   35|       |
   36|       |#define X25519_PRIVATE_KEY_LEN 32
   37|       |#define X25519_PUBLIC_VALUE_LEN 32
   38|       |#define X25519_SHARED_KEY_LEN 32
   39|       |
   40|       |// X25519_keypair sets `out_public_value` and `out_private_key` to a freshly
   41|       |// generated, public–private key pair.
   42|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void X25519_keypair(uint8_t out_public_value[32],
   43|       |                                   uint8_t out_private_key[32]);
   44|       |
   45|       |// X25519 writes a shared key to `out_shared_key` that is calculated from the
   46|       |// given private key and the peer's public value. It returns one on success and
   47|       |// zero on error.
   48|       |//
   49|       |// Don't use the shared key directly, rather use a KDF and also include the two
   50|       |// public values as inputs.
   51|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int X25519(uint8_t out_shared_key[32],
   52|       |                          const uint8_t private_key[32],
   53|       |                          const uint8_t peer_public_value[32]);
   54|       |
   55|       |// X25519_public_from_private calculates a Diffie-Hellman public value from the
   56|       |// given private key and writes it to `out_public_value`.
   57|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void X25519_public_from_private(uint8_t out_public_value[32],
   58|       |                                               const uint8_t private_key[32]);
   59|       |
   60|       |
   61|       |// Ed25519.
   62|       |//
   63|       |// Ed25519 is a signature scheme using a twisted-Edwards curve that is
   64|       |// birationally equivalent to curve25519.
   65|       |//
   66|       |// Note that, unlike RFC 8032's formulation, our private key representation
   67|       |// includes a public key suffix to make multiple key signing operations with the
   68|       |// same key more efficient. The RFC 8032 private key is referred to in this
   69|       |// implementation as the "seed" and is the first 32 bytes of our private key.
   70|       |
   71|       |#define ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY_LEN 64
   72|      0|#define ED25519_PUBLIC_KEY_LEN 32
   73|      0|#define ED25519_SIGNATURE_LEN 64
   74|       |
   75|       |// ED25519_keypair sets `out_public_key` and `out_private_key` to a freshly
   76|       |// generated, public–private key pair.
   77|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void ED25519_keypair(uint8_t out_public_key[32],
   78|       |                                    uint8_t out_private_key[64]);
   79|       |
   80|       |// ED25519_sign sets `out_sig` to be a signature of `message_len` bytes from
   81|       |// `message` using `private_key`. It returns one on success or zero on
   82|       |// allocation failure.
   83|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int ED25519_sign(uint8_t out_sig[64], const uint8_t *message,
   84|       |                                size_t message_len,
   85|       |                                const uint8_t private_key[64]);
   86|       |
   87|       |// ED25519_verify returns one iff `signature` is a valid signature, by
   88|       |// `public_key` of `message_len` bytes from `message`. It returns zero
   89|       |// otherwise.
   90|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int ED25519_verify(const uint8_t *message, size_t message_len,
   91|       |                                  const uint8_t signature[64],
   92|       |                                  const uint8_t public_key[32]);
   93|       |
   94|       |// ED25519_keypair_from_seed calculates a public and private key from an
   95|       |// Ed25519 “seed”. Seed values are not exposed by this API (although they
   96|       |// happen to be the first 32 bytes of a private key) so this function is for
   97|       |// interoperating with systems that may store just a seed instead of a full
   98|       |// private key.
   99|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void ED25519_keypair_from_seed(uint8_t out_public_key[32],
  100|       |                                              uint8_t out_private_key[64],
  101|       |                                              const uint8_t seed[32]);
  102|       |
  103|       |
  104|       |// SPAKE2.
  105|       |//
  106|       |// SPAKE2 is a password-authenticated key-exchange. It allows two parties,
  107|       |// who share a low-entropy secret (i.e. password), to agree on a shared key.
  108|       |// An attacker can only make one guess of the password per execution of the
  109|       |// protocol.
  110|       |//
  111|       |// See https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-irtf-cfrg-spake2-02.
  112|       |
  113|       |// spake2_role_t enumerates the different “roles” in SPAKE2. The protocol
  114|       |// requires that the symmetry of the two parties be broken so one participant
  115|       |// must be “Alice” and the other be “Bob”.
  116|       |enum spake2_role_t {
  117|       |  spake2_role_alice,
  118|       |  spake2_role_bob,
  119|       |};
  120|       |
  121|       |// SPAKE2_CTX_new creates a new `SPAKE2_CTX` (which can only be used for a
  122|       |// single execution of the protocol). SPAKE2 requires the symmetry of the two
  123|       |// parties to be broken which is indicated via `my_role` – each party must pass
  124|       |// a different value for this argument.
  125|       |//
  126|       |// The `my_name` and `their_name` arguments allow optional, opaque names to be
  127|       |// bound into the protocol. For example MAC addresses, hostnames, usernames
  128|       |// etc. These values are not exposed and can avoid context-confusion attacks
  129|       |// when a password is shared between several devices.
  130|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT SPAKE2_CTX *SPAKE2_CTX_new(
  131|       |    enum spake2_role_t my_role,
  132|       |    const uint8_t *my_name, size_t my_name_len,
  133|       |    const uint8_t *their_name, size_t their_name_len);
  134|       |
  135|       |// SPAKE2_CTX_free frees `ctx` and all the resources that it has allocated.
  136|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void SPAKE2_CTX_free(SPAKE2_CTX *ctx);
  137|       |
  138|       |// SPAKE2_MAX_MSG_SIZE is the maximum size of a SPAKE2 message.
  139|       |#define SPAKE2_MAX_MSG_SIZE 32
  140|       |
  141|       |// SPAKE2_generate_msg generates a SPAKE2 message given `password`, writes
  142|       |// it to `out` and sets `*out_len` to the number of bytes written.
  143|       |//
  144|       |// At most `max_out_len` bytes are written to `out` and, in order to ensure
  145|       |// success, `max_out_len` should be at least `SPAKE2_MAX_MSG_SIZE` bytes.
  146|       |//
  147|       |// This function can only be called once for a given `SPAKE2_CTX`.
  148|       |//
  149|       |// It returns one on success and zero on error.
  150|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SPAKE2_generate_msg(SPAKE2_CTX *ctx, uint8_t *out,
  151|       |                                       size_t *out_len, size_t max_out_len,
  152|       |                                       const uint8_t *password,
  153|       |                                       size_t password_len);
  154|       |
  155|       |// SPAKE2_MAX_KEY_SIZE is the maximum amount of key material that SPAKE2 will
  156|       |// produce.
  157|       |#define SPAKE2_MAX_KEY_SIZE 64
  158|       |
  159|       |// SPAKE2_process_msg completes the SPAKE2 exchange given the peer's message in
  160|       |// `their_msg`, writes at most `max_out_key_len` bytes to `out_key` and sets
  161|       |// `*out_key_len` to the number of bytes written.
  162|       |//
  163|       |// The resulting keying material is suitable for:
  164|       |//    - Using directly in a key-confirmation step: i.e. each side could
  165|       |//      transmit a hash of their role, a channel-binding value and the key
  166|       |//      material to prove to the other side that they know the shared key.
  167|       |//   -  Using as input keying material to HKDF to generate a variety of subkeys
  168|       |//      for encryption etc.
  169|       |//
  170|       |// If `max_out_key_key` is smaller than the amount of key material generated
  171|       |// then the key is silently truncated. If you want to ensure that no truncation
  172|       |// occurs then `max_out_key` should be at least `SPAKE2_MAX_KEY_SIZE`.
  173|       |//
  174|       |// You must call `SPAKE2_generate_msg` on a given `SPAKE2_CTX` before calling
  175|       |// this function. On successful return, `ctx` is complete and calling
  176|       |// `SPAKE2_CTX_free` is the only acceptable operation on it.
  177|       |//
  178|       |// Returns one on success or zero on error.
  179|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SPAKE2_process_msg(SPAKE2_CTX *ctx, uint8_t *out_key,
  180|       |                                      size_t *out_key_len,
  181|       |                                      size_t max_out_key_len,
  182|       |                                      const uint8_t *their_msg,
  183|       |                                      size_t their_msg_len);
  184|       |
  185|       |
  186|       |#if defined(__cplusplus)
  187|       |}  // extern C
  188|       |
  189|       |extern "C++" {
  190|       |
  191|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
  192|       |
  193|       |BORINGSSL_MAKE_DELETER(SPAKE2_CTX, SPAKE2_CTX_free)
  194|       |
  195|       |BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
  196|       |
  197|       |}  // extern C++
  198|       |
  199|       |#endif
  200|       |
  201|       |#endif  // OPENSSL_HEADER_CURVE25519_H

   15|       |#ifndef OPENSSL_HEADER_SHA2_H
   16|       |#define OPENSSL_HEADER_SHA2_H
   17|       |

   19|       |

   25|       |// The SHA-2 family of hash functions.
   26|       |
   27|       |
   28|       |// SHA-224.
   29|       |
   30|       |// SHA224_CBLOCK is the block size of SHA-224.
   31|       |#define SHA224_CBLOCK 64
   32|       |
   33|       |// SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH is the length of a SHA-224 digest.
   34|      0|#define SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH 28
   35|       |
   36|       |// SHA224_Init initialises `sha` and returns 1.
   37|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA224_Init(SHA256_CTX *sha);
   38|       |
   39|       |// SHA224_Update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha` and returns 1.
   40|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA224_Update(SHA256_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
   41|       |
   42|       |// SHA224_Final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting digest
   43|       |// to `out`, which must have at least `SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of space. It
   44|       |// returns 1.
   45|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA224_Final(uint8_t out[SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH],
   46|       |                                SHA256_CTX *sha);
   47|       |
   48|       |// SHA224 writes the digest of `len` bytes from `data` to `out` and returns
   49|       |// `out`. There must be at least `SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of space in
   50|       |// `out`.
   51|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT uint8_t *SHA224(const uint8_t *data, size_t len,
   52|       |                               uint8_t out[SHA224_DIGEST_LENGTH]);
   53|       |
   54|       |
   55|       |// SHA-256.
   56|       |
   57|       |// SHA256_CBLOCK is the block size of SHA-256.
   58|       |#define SHA256_CBLOCK 64
   59|       |
   60|       |// SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH is the length of a SHA-256 digest.
   61|   160k|#define SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH 32
   62|       |
   63|       |// SHA256_Init initialises `sha` and returns 1.
   64|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA256_Init(SHA256_CTX *sha);
   65|       |
   66|       |// SHA256_Update adds `len` bytes from `data` to `sha` and returns 1.
   67|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA256_Update(SHA256_CTX *sha, const void *data, size_t len);
   68|       |
   69|       |// SHA256_Final adds the final padding to `sha` and writes the resulting digest
   70|       |// to `out`, which must have at least `SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of space. It
   71|       |// returns one on success and zero on programmer error.
   72|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT int SHA256_Final(uint8_t out[SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH],
   73|       |                                SHA256_CTX *sha);
   74|       |
   75|       |// SHA256 writes the digest of `len` bytes from `data` to `out` and returns
   76|       |// `out`. There must be at least `SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH` bytes of space in
   77|       |// `out`.
   78|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT uint8_t *SHA256(const uint8_t *data, size_t len,
   79|       |                               uint8_t out[SHA256_DIGEST_LENGTH]);
   80|       |
   81|       |// SHA256_Transform is a low-level function that performs a single, SHA-256
   82|       |// block transformation using the state from `sha` and `SHA256_CBLOCK` bytes
   83|       |// from `block`.
   84|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void SHA256_Transform(SHA256_CTX *sha,
   85|       |                                     const uint8_t block[SHA256_CBLOCK]);
   86|       |
   87|       |// SHA256_TransformBlocks is a low-level function that takes `num_blocks` *
   88|       |// `SHA256_CBLOCK` bytes of data and performs SHA-256 transforms on it to update
   89|       |// `state`. You should not use this function unless you are implementing a
   90|       |// derivative of SHA-256.
   91|       |OPENSSL_EXPORT void SHA256_TransformBlocks(uint32_t state[8],
   92|       |                                           const uint8_t *data,
   93|       |                                           size_t num_blocks);
   94|       |
   95|       |struct sha256_state_st {
   96|       |  uint32_t h[8];
   97|       |  uint32_t Nl, Nh;
   98|       |  uint8_t data[SHA256_CBLOCK];
   99|       |  unsigned num, md_len;
  100|       |} /* SHA256_CTX */;
  101|       |

  103|       |// SHA-384.
  104|       |

  106|       |#define SHA384_CBLOCK 128

  109|      0|#define SHA384_DIGEST_LENGTH 48

   48|       |#if defined(__cpp_lib_ranges) && __cpp_lib_ranges >= 201911L
   49|       |// Mark `Span` as satisfying the `view` and `borrowed_range` concepts. This
   50|       |// should be done before the definition of `Span`, so that any inlined calls to
   51|       |// range functionality use the correct specializations.
   52|       |template <typename T, size_t N>
   53|       |inline constexpr bool std::ranges::enable_view<bssl::Span<T, N>> = true;
   54|       |template <typename T, size_t N>

   56|       |    true;
